confirmed.title: write_patched_pfile does not validate header_size >= 128, producing silent header corruption or an uncatchable struct.error on a corrupt/hostile source
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code on this branch and both failure modes reproduced empirically.

CODE: write_patched_pfile (config_patch.py:504-528) reads header_size verbatim from header word 17 (line 504) and uses it unchecked to (a) f.read(header_size) into header_region (line 527) and (b) struct.pack_into at byte offset config_size*2 = 22 (line 528). I confirmed via import that _H["config_size"]=11 -> offset 22, requiring buffer >= 24. The function HAS guards for version<6 (507), config>65535 (511), and first_record_size >= file_size (521), but NO header_size < HEADER_BYTES guard. The sibling reader extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138) DOES have exactly that guard (`if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError`), so the finding's parity argument is sound.

REPRODUCED: With a synthetic header (endian flag word 63 = 1, version major 6, file_size 10000):
- header_size=20, config_size=0: raises `struct.error: pack_into requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes for packing 2 bytes at offset 22 (actual buffer size is 20)`. Confirmed struct.error MRO is [error, Exception, BaseException, object] — NOT OSError, NOT ValueError. The patch-config CLI handler (cli.py:291) catches only (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError), so this escapes as an uncaught traceback with wrong exit behavior. Confirmed accurate.
- header_size=64, config_size=0: SUCCEEDS, writes a 10009-byte .p whose header region is only 64 bytes (config text begins at offset 64) — a silently malformed/unreadable .p. Confirmed accurate.

REACHABILITY/PRACTICAL IMPACT: Path is reachable via patch_files -> write_patched_pfile (line 657). However it requires a corrupt/hostile .p whose header simultaneously has a valid endian flag, version major>=6, header_size<128, and file_size>first_record_size — conditions that never hold for a genuine RSI .p file (always header_size=128). Additionally, read_config_text returns an empty config for such a header, so the edit spec must actually produce changes on that empty config (typically via --add-keys) to reach the write at all; otherwise the file is skipped at line 642. So this is an untrusted-input / off-nominal robustness gap, not a wrong-result-on-valid-runs defect. Not covered by tests (test_config_patch.py guards other corrupt-header cases but not this one). NOT fixed by PR #78 (whose 4 fixes are in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py; this function is untouched). Severity 'minor' is correctly calibrated: the silent-malformed-output half (mode 2) is real output corruption, keeping it above nit, but it only triggers on corrupt/hostile input, keeping it below major.

confirmed.title: edit_config_text mis-detects a pre-existing CONFIG_MARKER inside the ORIGINAL config, silently freezing everything below it as an 'original block' and refusing edits with a misleading 'section is not present' error
confirmed.reasoning: The mechanism exists: edit_config_text (config_patch.py:324-330) splits at config_str.find(CONFIG_MARKER) and re-attaches everything from the FIRST marker occurrence verbatim, never parsing it. I reproduced the cited ValueError for a config that literally contains '; ### Original Configuration String Below ###' above a [cruise_info] stanza: edit raises "[cruise_info] section is not present in this file" (config_patch.py:390). So scenario B holds and is a genuine latent edge case — hence real=true, not not-a-bug. BUT the finding is overstated on its primary justification. I read RSI patch_setupstr.m (lines 318-354): RSI builds the patched string as [active config] then appends, below the marker, a FULLY ';'-commented archival copy of the original (line 324 sprintf '%s\n; %s'). The real/active stanzas live ABOVE the marker. I verified empirically that editing the cruise_info of an RSI-patched file SUCCEEDS (changes applied). Therefore the headline claim — that "a .p ALREADY patched by RSI's MATLAB tool ... will have all [channel]/[instrument_info]/[cruise_info] stanzas located BELOW the marker rendered invisible" — is FALSE; RSI's editable stanzas are above the marker, and the tool handles them correctly. That leaves only the second, contrived sub-case: an original/unpatched config that itself contains the marker string verbatim. That string is emitted only by patch tooling, never by acquisition; the real ARCTERX config (VMP/*.p, 10371-byte config) contains no marker and no '###' lines at all. The failure is reachable only via the new opt-in patch-config tool (not the default eps/chi processing path), and it raises a loud ValueError rather than silently corrupting output — so the "silent data-loss" / "silently impossible" framing is misleading. The test-gap sub-claim is accurate: tests/test_config_patch.py:359 covers only the self-produced idempotent re-patch, not a marker-in-original case. Net: a latent robustness nit on adversarial/synthetic input with an incorrect interoperability rationale, not a minor. Downgraded blocker/major/minor -> nit. Not fixed by PR #78 (which touches chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py; config_patch.py is from PR #77 and unchanged here).

confirmed.title: Production epsilon viscosity ignores salinity and pressure (visc35/S=35 hardcoded), inconsistent with chi/N2 viscosity in the same pipeline run
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Every load-bearing claim holds:

1. l4.py:176 computes per-window viscosity as `nu = float(visc35(temp[j]))` — temperature-only, S=35 hardcoded, pressure-independent. l4.py imports ONLY `visc35` (line 21), not `visc`, and `process_l4` (docstring at lines 100-129) has no salinity/pressure parameter, so it cannot do salinity-aware viscosity even structurally.

2. The deprecated dissipation.py path DOES support salinity-aware epsilon viscosity: dissipation.py:212-220 computes `nu_out = visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)` when salinity is provided, falling back to visc35 otherwise. So the production path is a genuine regression in capability relative to the deprecated one. L3Data carries per-window `pres` (l3.py:177,270), so the data needed is available and discarded.

3. The silent within-run inconsistency is real: run_pipeline calls `process_l4(l3, temp=l3.temp, ...)` for EPSILON at pipeline.py:347 (no salinity) BEFORE resolving `chi_salinity, measured_sal = _resolve_salinity(l1, salinity)` at pipeline.py:361. That resolved salinity (measured JAC C/T when available, per _resolve_salinity at pipeline.py:69-70) is then fed to chi viscosity (salinity=chi_salinity, pipeline.py:389) and to N2 stratification (S=chi_salinity, pipeline.py:453). The target instrument (VMP-250IR SN479) has JAC conductivity, so a real field run uses measured S for chi+N2 but S=35 for epsilon — exactly the divergence claimed.

4. The quantified bias was reproduced via ocean.visc vs ocean.visc35 at T=10C: +0.053% at S=30,P=0 and +0.762% at S=37,P=500dbar, matching the finding's ~0.05%/~0.76% figures. Epsilon scales ~linearly with nu, so the epsilon bias is sub-1% in realistic conditions.

5. PR #78 (commit 1a78fca) does not touch l4.py/pipeline.py/dissipation.py viscosity, so the defect persists on this branch.

Severity remains minor (not nit): the practical numerical impact is sub-percent (well below the factor-of-2 uncertainty of microstructure epsilon), but it is a legitimate silent inconsistency in field-data output and a documented capability regression from the deprecated path — a real correctness/consistency concern a maintainer would want to fix, not merely cosmetic. The finding is honest and not overstated: it self-limits to "the SILENT inconsistency, not the benchmark value" and correctly notes visc35 must stay as the ATOMIX-benchmark-pinned default. Not a blocker/major because it does not produce a wrong primary result on every valid run, nor cause silent loss/corruption of output at any material magnitude.

confirmed.title: _compute_epsilon lacks the NaN-temperature viscosity guard that process_l4 has, silently producing NaN epsilon
confirmed.reasoning: The asymmetry is REAL on this branch and NOT fixed by PR #78 (commit 1a78fca does not touch dissipation.py; there is no isfinite/nan_to_num/10.0 temp guard anywhere in the file). process_l4 (scor160/l4.py:154-160) does `if not np.all(np.isfinite(temp))` -> warn + substitute 10 degC, whereas _compute_epsilon (dissipation.py:218-220) calls `visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)` / `visc35(l3.temp)` with no guard. I verified numerically: visc35(nan)=nan, visc(nan,35,100)=nan, and nasmyth_grid(eps, nu=nan, k) returns all-NaN WITHOUT raising (the `if nu <= 0` check at nasmyth.py:192 is False for NaN). So if l3.temp were NaN, epsilon and the Nasmyth spectrum would be silently all-NaN with no warning. HOWEVER the finding is materially overstated and misframed, justifying a downgrade from minor to nit: (1) It calls _compute_epsilon a 'deprecated' path contrasted with 'the maintained pipeline'; that is backwards. _compute_epsilon is the actively-maintained worker behind compute_diss_file -> the `rsi-tpw eps` CLI (cli.py:353) and the perturb campaign pipeline (perturb/pipeline.py:1241); process_l4 is the OTHER path (rsi/pipeline.py:347 run_pipeline + scor160 benchmark). (2) The finding's cited NaN-source mechanism (scor160/l3.py:181 appending np.nan when temp is absent/shape-mismatched) does NOT apply to this path: _build_l1data_from_channels (helpers.py:436) always sets `temp=T_fast[s_fast:e_fast]`, full-length and shape-matched to n_time, so l3.py:178's `l1.temp.size > 0 and l1.temp.shape[0] == l1.n_time` check passes and the NaN-append branch at line 181 is never reached. (3) For the defect to manifest, T_slow (= data['T'], the measured slow temperature channel from `pf.channels[t_name]` or an NC variable, helpers.py:133/214) must itself contain NaN, since T_fast = np.interp(t_fast, t_slow, T_slow) (helpers.py:331). In the documented default .p-file workflow T_slow is a real measured temperature time series and is not NaN, and the finding demonstrates no realistic path by which NaN reaches l3.temp here. Net: a genuine but latent defensive-coding inconsistency (a robustness guard present in a sibling path, absent here) that bites only on an off-nominal NaN temperature channel not produced by the standard run -- a nit, not a minor defect that corrupts real output on a valid default run.

confirmed.title: rsi pipeline binned/combined products (L5/L6) have no units, long_name, or standard_name on any data variable
confirmed.reasoning: Verified in current branch code and at runtime. (1) bin_by_depth (binning.py:103-122) attaches only {'cell_methods': ...} (plus a 'comment' on the geometric-mean path) to each data variable — no units/long_name/standard_name. Runtime: bin_by_depth(...)['epsilon'].attrs == {'cell_methods': 'depth_bin: mean'}. (2) combine_profiles builds data_vars[var] = (['profile','depth_bin'], combined) with NO attrs tuple element (combine.py:82), so even cell_methods is dropped. Runtime: combine_profiles(...)['epsilon'].attrs == {}. (3) These are the documented headline products: L5_binned.nc written from the merged bin_by_depth output (pipeline.py:599) and L6_combined.nc from combine_profiles (pipeline.py:279), both produced by run_pipeline. Neither path adds per-variable units downstream. (4) The asymmetry claim holds: _write_l4_epsilon/_write_l4_chi attach units/long_name/standard_name to every variable (pipeline.py:637-783), and mixing_vars even carries a fully-populated attrs dict (units/long_name/comment at pipeline.py:477-531) that is explicitly discarded by the binning call site `{name: arr for name, (arr, _a) in mixing_vars.items()}` (pipeline.py:581). (5) Test-gap claim holds: tests assert only the depth_bin COORDINATE units (test_binning_combine.py test_depth_bin_coord / test_depth_bin_attributes), with no assertion on epsilon/chi data-var units. PR #78 does not touch binning.py/combine.py/this pipeline path, so it is not fixed by this branch. CONCLUSION: the defect is real and worth fixing. But I down-rate severity from major to minor: the numerical values in L5/L6 are fully intact and correct — only CF metadata attributes are missing. Per the rubric, major = silent corruption/loss of real output; no output data is lost or corrupted here. This is a metadata/CF-compliance quality gap (and an internal inconsistency vs the L4 files), i.e. off-nominal/minor, not major.

confirmed.title: L6_combined.nc and L5_binned.nc lack the Conventions/CF global attribute set by every other writer
confirmed.reasoning: Factually confirmed on this branch. combine.py:89-107 builds the L6 result Dataset and only writes per-variable coord attrs (depth_bin units/standard_name/positive at lines 90-98) and profile_N_* metadata strings (lines 101-105); result.attrs never gets a "Conventions" key, and pipeline.py:277-279 calls combined.to_netcdf with no further attrs. For L5, pipeline.py:595-597 sets only profile_number/source_file/bin_size before binned.to_netcdf at line 599. By contrast every sibling/headline product sets Conventions="CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3": convert.py:478, profile.py:148/304, dissipation.py:583, chi_io.py:666, the L4 epsilon writer (pipeline.py:730), and the L4 chi writer (pipeline.py:842). PR #78 only touches chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py, so this is not fixed by #78, and grep of tests shows no test asserts Conventions on the L5/L6 binned/combined outputs (no duplicate prior fix). So the defect genuinely holds. However severity "minor" is overstated: this is purely a global-metadata omission. No numeric result is wrong, no real output is lost or silently corrupted, and the data variables/coordinates already carry correct CF per-variable units/standard_name/positive attributes. The only consequence is that a strict CF/ACDD validator will not auto-validate these two files. That is cosmetic/latent metadata polish, i.e. a nit, not a minor data-quality issue.

confirmed.title: L1 supplementary channels written with raw RSI units bypassing canonicalize_units
confirmed.reasoning: Mechanism is real and verified. convert.py:541 writes `v.units = info["units"]` verbatim in the supplementary-channel loop, while the per-profile writer canonicalizes (profile.py:244-246 calls canonicalize_units from netcdf_schema). The raw converter units are as claimed: convert_inclxy->'deg' (channels.py:220), convert_jac_c->'mS_cm-1' (:242), convert_aroft_o2->'umol_L-1' (:265), convert_inclt->'deg_C' (:228), and UNITS_FIXUP translates all of them (netcdf_schema.py:24-33). I empirically confirmed cf_units REJECTS 'deg'/'mS_cm-1'/'umol_L-1' but ACCEPTS 'deg_C'. Not fixed by PR #78 (its fix commit 1a78fca does not touch convert.py; the 'convert' string in its stat was prose about ct_align). canonicalize_units has never been applied in convert.py (git -S search empty), so not a duplicate of a prior fix. test_cf_compliance (test_convert.py:149-177) does no UDUNITS parse, so it is uncaught — test-gap claim accurate.\n\nHOWEVER the practical impact is overstated, justifying a downgrade from minor to nit: On the actual SN479 default product (`rsi-tpw nc`), _classify_channels yields supplementary = ['Gnd','Incl_T','PV','V_Bat'] with units counts/deg_C/unknown/V — every one of which cf_units ACCEPTS. So the documented primary product on the real campaign data emits ZERO UDUNITS-invalid units from this loop. The finding even concedes this ('Incl_T=deg_C is borderline'). Worse, its headline example is wrong: Incl_X/Incl_Y (inclxy type) are routed to NAMED pitch/roll variables with hardcoded valid 'degrees' (convert.py:71-77, specs lines 332/346), NOT to the supplementary loop, so they never leak 'deg'. The only genuine hard-invalid leak path is off-nominal configs absent from SN479 — a third inclxy channel whose name lacks 'X'/'Y', a SECOND aroft_o2 (doxy_name uses next(), only the first is mapped), or a second jac_t. These are latent/hypothetical, not silent corruption of any real output. So: real defect (a true inconsistency between two writers plus a missing test), but latent/cosmetic on every supported config = nit.

confirmed.title: L1 MAG variable declares units='micro_Tesla', not UDUNITS-parseable
confirmed.reasoning: Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. convert.py line 360 (JSON cites 361; the units key sits at 360 inside the MAG spec block 352-364) sets units="micro_Tesla" for the MAG L1 variable. cf_units 3.3.1 confirms cf_units.Unit('micro_Tesla') raises ValueError [UT_UNKNOWN] "Failed to parse unit", whereas 'uT', 'microtesla', and 'microTesla' all parse — so the string is genuinely not UDUNITS/CF-parseable. The attr is written verbatim: _create_l1_variables (line 134-140) does setattr(v, key, val) for each attr with NO canonicalization, and the only canonicalize_units path (perturb/netcdf_schema.py UNITS_FIXUP, applied in rsi/profile.py:245) (a) only covers extract-time passthrough channels, not convert.py L1 specs, and (b) has no 'micro_Tesla' entry anyway — matching the evidence's "canonicalize_units gap above." The suggested-fix note that 'magnetic_field' is not a CF standard name is also correct (the CF table has no bare 'magnetic_field'). PR #78's four fixes (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py CT, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py) do not touch this. HOWEVER: the MAG variable is gated by `_stack(mag_names) if mag_names else None`, and the spec is skipped when data is None (line 135-136). I confirmed the actual SN479/ARCTERX data has zero 'magn'-type channels (PFile over VMP/*.p), so the variable is NEVER emitted for this dataset — the defect is fully latent and cannot affect any current run; it would only manifest for a magnetometer-equipped instrument, and even then only produces a non-parseable metadata string (failing CF-checkers / cf_units consumers), not numeric corruption or any change to epsilon/chi output. Real but latent and metadata-only, so I downgrade minor -> nit.

confirmed.title: CTD per-file NetCDF writes _FillValue on the 'time' coordinate variable (CF-1.13 §2.5.1 violation)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified the code and the technical claim on this branch. ctd.py:375-378 builds `time` as an xarray float coordinate and ctd.py:419 calls `ds.to_netcdf(out_path)` with no `encoding=`. I reproduced the behavior empirically: writing such a Dataset with netCDF4/xarray yields `time` ncattrs `['_FillValue', 'units', 'calendar']` — i.e. xarray auto-emits `_FillValue` on the time coordinate, which CF-1.13 §2.5.1 forbids on coordinate variables. The contrast claim is also accurate: combo.py:283-291 explicitly strips this exact thing for exactly this reason (`encoding[cname] = {'_FillValue': None}` for every coord before `combo.to_netcdf`). And the mitigation claim holds: make_combo opens inputs with `decode_times=False` (combo.py:83) and re-writes the combo with the FillValue-stripping encoding, so the final CTD combo.nc is CF-clean; only the intermediate per-file `ctd/*.nc` products (produced on the real pipeline path, pipeline.py:1111) carry the non-compliant attribute. So the defect genuinely holds on this branch. However the original `minor` severity is overstated. There is no data corruption, no crash, and no wrong scientific result: `_FillValue` on a dense coordinate variable is purely a convention/metadata violation that a strict CF checker flags as a warning while essentially all readers (including xarray's own round-trip) tolerate it, and the value is never actually used as a fill because the coordinate is fully populated. The non-compliant attribute also never reaches the final deliverable. That is cosmetic/latent metadata on an intermediate product — a nit, not a minor.

confirmed.title: PR #78 _nc_filled fix: one mid-cast _FillValue in slow pressure NaNs the entire fall-rate (gradient+filtfilt), so zero profiles are detected for that .nc input
confirmed.reasoning: Technical core CONFIRMED. _nc_filled (profile.py:342, the PR #78 M2 fix) converts masked/_FillValue slow-pressure entries to NaN. smooth_fall_rate (scor160/profile.py:58-61) does np.gradient(P) then filtfilt; I reproduced that a single NaN in a 2000-sample P yields 0/2000 finite W, and get_profiles then returns [] (vs [(10,1999)] clean). End-to-end via the real _load_from_nc -> _smooth_fall_rate -> get_profiles chain: one mid-cast fill -> 0 profiles -> file dropped. The M2 test TestM2FillValueLeak (tests/test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:88-98) only asserts P[5]==NaN and never calls extract_profiles, so this downstream behavior is genuinely uncovered. HOWEVER, the finding overstates severity from major to minor: (1) Reachability is narrow — _nc_filled is only hit via _load_from_nc, i.e. only when a user feeds a full-record .nc to the rsi CLI (cli.py:330-332). The primary perturb pipeline (pipeline.py:1174) and the .p path both go through _load_from_pfile, which never touches _nc_filled and produces no masked pressure. (2) No real file triggers it: convert.py never sets _FillValue and fully writes P; I checked every allch.nc in MR/ and VMP/ — zero _FillValue on slow pressure, zero masked entries. The trigger requires an external CF/ATOMIX file with both a declared _FillValue on slow pressure AND a real mid-cast gap, none present in-repo. (3) It is NOT silent: extract_profiles logs logger.warning('No profiles found in {stem}') at profile.py:127 and skips the file. (4) The 'worse than before' framing is debatable: pre-fix the raw 9.97e36 fill produced a partial profile [(10,1000)] but wrote fill garbage into the profile pressure/channels (silent corruption feeding epsilon/chi); a logged file-drop is arguably safer. So: real, fixable (interpolate/repair isolated NaNs in P and warn; add an extract_profiles end-to-end test), but an off-nominal edge affecting only external .nc inputs with a visible warning — minor, not a major silent-corruption regression on the default path.

confirmed.title: extract_profiles crashes with AttributeError on external/partial CF/ATOMIX NetCDF whose channel carries a _FillValue attribute — the very files PR #78's _nc_filled fix claims to support
confirmed.line: 246 (blind setattr), fed by ncattrs() copy at 440-442; createVariable without fill_value at 240
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Code reads exactly as described. _scan_vars (profile.py:440-442) copies every var.ncattrs() entry — including _FillValue — into each channel's attrs dict. The per-profile write loop creates the new variable WITHOUT a fill_value kwarg (line 240: `var = ds.createVariable(var_name, "f4", (dim,), zlib=True)`) and then does `setattr(var, k, v)` for every attr (line 246). netCDF4 forbids assigning _FillValue after variable creation.

Reproduced end-to-end: I built a full-record root-format .nc with a detectable descending/ascending pressure cast and a fast channel `T1` created with `fill_value=-999.0`, then called the real extract_profiles(). It crashed at profile.py:246 with the exact `AttributeError: _FillValue attribute must be set when variable is created (using fill_value keyword to createVariable)`. An isolated netCDF4 1.7.4 repro of the setattr also raises the same error. The schema-overwrite loop at 249-251 is never reached because line 246 raises first (confirmed by traceback).

Not fixed by PR #78. PR #78's _nc_filled (line 330-342) only fixed the READ side (masked->NaN) and its docstring (lines 339-340) explicitly advertises support for "external / partial (CF/ATOMIX) files that declare a _FillValue". The WRITE side (extract_profiles) still aborts on those very files, so the fix's stated support class is exactly the crashing case — not a duplicate/already-handled issue.

Why tests miss it: the package's own writers (convert.py, p_file.py) never set _FillValue (grep: zero hits), so its .p->.nc->profile round-trip never carries _FillValue. The test helper tests/test_rsi_profile.py _write_nc creates channels sh1/SHEAR WITHOUT fill_value, so no extract_profiles test exercises the crash. The audit-fixes test (tests/test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:79-93) does put a _FillValue, but only on P (pressure) and only exercises the READ path _load_from_nc — and P is read separately (line 408) and never enters the channels list, so it never hits the line-246 write path.

Severity major is correct, not blocker: the default/primary path is .p files (no _FillValue), so this is not a crash on every valid run; it is a crash on the explicitly-supported external CF/ATOMIX .nc input class, where declaring _FillValue on data variables is standard CF practice and the L1_converted/ATOMIX group path is documented-supported (lines 447-449). Off-nominal-but-supported-input crash with no graceful handling = major. Suggested fix (skip reserved attrs like _FillValue/_Netcdf4Coordinates/_Netcdf4Dimid/_Unsigned when copying, or pass fill_value through to createVariable) is sound.

confirmed.title: PR #78 _variance_correction uses a flat 80,000-point grid (n_fine floor is dead), ~22x slower per call than needed with no accuracy benefit
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified against the current branch code at chi.py:112-114. The finding's factual claims all hold:

1. Grid size: _N_FINE=2000 (line 62), _K_SPAN_KB=40 (line 69), _PTS_PER_KB=2000 (line 70). Line 113 computes n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB*_PTS_PER_KB) = max(2000, 80000) = 80000 unconditionally. The n_fine floor is indeed dead (2000 is always dominated by 80000).

2. PR #78 origin: git log -L confirms the prior code (commit d3395b3 era) was n_fine=2000 points with K_upper=max(K_max*5, kB*5) and np.linspace; commit 1a78fca (the PR #78 audit fix on this branch) replaced it with the 80000-point arange grid. So this is genuinely a PR #78 change.

3. Timing: benchmarked _variance_correction at 0.87 ms/call (current, 80000 pts) vs 0.04 ms/call (emulated old, 2000 pts) = ~21.7x, matching the ~22x claim.

4. Call frequency: _variance_correction is called inside the up-to-3-iteration refinement loop (chi.py:544 for-loop, call at 588), once per chi window per probe per profile. No caching is present.

5. Accuracy/overhead claim: I confirmed that a 10x-coarser grid (_PTS_PER_KB=200, n=8000) reproduces the 80000-point correction value to <3.8e-5 relative error across kB in [2,30], and pts=500 to <6e-6. So the steep peak near kB is resolved by the dK spacing regardless of total point count — the 80000 floor is mostly overhead, exactly as the finding states.

However, the practical impact is smaller than 'minor' implies. The regression has NO output consequence: the correction value at 80000 pts (1.033123) vs the old grid (1.033194) differs by ~7e-5, far below any meaningful chi precision, and the change does not crash or silently corrupt anything. The absolute wall-clock cost is tiny: ~0.83 ms extra per call, on the order of a few seconds added across a full 30-profile, 2-probe ARCTERX campaign, where FFT/spectral estimation dominates runtime. Per the rubric (nit = cosmetic/latent, no functional consequence; minor = off-nominal/edge with real effect), this is best classified as a nit: a real but consequence-free CPU inefficiency that the code itself already documents the floor for. The defect is real (the inefficiency exists exactly as described) but I downgrade severity minor->nit.

confirmed.title: convert_all flattens input paths to basename under output_dir, so same-named .p files in different directories collide on one .nc (parallel race / silent overwrite)
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL. convert.py:592 computes `nc_path = output_dir / pf_path.with_suffix(".nc").name`, using only the basename. I reproduced the collision: VMP/a/SN479_0001.p and VMP/b/SN479_0001.p both map to out/SN479_0001.nc. The `work` list (588-595) has no dedup or collision check. With jobs>1 (607-617) the ProcessPoolExecutor workers race-write the same path -> partial/corrupt NetCDF; with jobs=1 (600-606) the later file silently overwrites the earlier with no warning. The path is reachable: cli.py:212-223 `_cmd_nc` passes `_resolve_p_files(args.files)` (cli.py:31-47, nargs="+", glob expansion) straight into convert_all, so `rsi-tpw nc dirA/*.p dirB/*.p -o out/` reaches it. NOT fixed by PR #78: `git diff main...HEAD` shows the 4 audit fixes touch chi/chi.py, processing/bottom.py, processing/ct_align.py, rsi/profile.py and tests — convert.py is untouched. Corroboration that this is a legitimate concern (not theoretical): the perturb subsystem explicitly tests this exact duplicate-basename collision (tests/test_perturb_pipeline.py:404, 453, 1065) and guards against it, but convert_all/`rsi-tpw nc` has no equivalent guard; test_convert.py:129-146 only convert a single file so never exercise it. SEVERITY: keeping minor (not raising to major). The default and only ARCTERX layout is a single flat VMP/ directory (no subdirs; ls VMP/*/ empty) with uniquely sequence-numbered files (ARCTERX_..._SN479_NNNN.p), so neither the silent-overwrite nor the parallel-race path is reachable on any default or representative run. It only fires when a user deliberately constructs a cross-directory input list with colliding basenames — off-nominal/edge input. The finding itself concedes the current layout doesn't trigger it. Real defect, but minor.

confirmed.title: parse_config raises a bare ValueError on a corrupt matrix row or channel id token, with no file context
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against the actual code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Line 249 reads `result["matrix"].append([int(x) for x in val.split()])` and line 390 reads `ids = [int(x) for x in ch["id"].replace(",", " ").split()]`, both applying int() directly to tokens from the embedded ASCII config string with no try/except. parse_config is invoked at line 313 inside PFile.__init__, immediately after the config bytes are decoded with errors="replace" (line 312), and there is no surrounding error handling that would add file/section context. So a non-numeric token in a matrix `row=` line or a channel `id=` field would propagate a bare `ValueError: invalid literal for int()` and crash the whole PFile read with no filename or section to localize the fault. The finding's claim that this is the config-string analogue of other diagnostics gaps, handled differently from graceful paths, is confirmed by line 530-532 where an unknown converter type only warns (warnings.warn) rather than crashing. The defect genuinely holds. However it correctly remains a nit: it never affects a valid run, never silently corrupts or loses output (it fails loud), and only manifests on corrupt/malformed config input — purely an error-message quality issue. PR #78 does not modify p_file.py config parsing, so it is not fixed by the PR, and it is a distinct parse path from the binary-header diagnostics findings, so not a duplicate. Severity left at nit.

confirmed.title: Edit-spec keys are never validated, allowing config-stanza injection that defeats the unaddressable-acquisition-params guarantee
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. PR #78 did not touch config_patch.py (git log main..HEAD shows no commits for this file; only #77 introduced it), so the defect is live.

The asymmetry is exactly as described. _check_value() (lines 111-138) rigorously rejects values containing non-ASCII, ';', and any control char < 0x20 (newlines), explicitly to stop [root]/[matrix] stanza injection. But _check_keymap() (lines 146-152) runs that validation only on the VALUE: it does `key = str(key)`, checks only `key.strip().lower() == RESERVED_KEY` ('name'), then `out[key] = _check_value(value, ...)`. The key text itself is never validated and is later written verbatim at line 386: `inserts...append((provenance(ch), f"{key} = {new_value}{eol}"))`.

I reproduced the injection end-to-end through the public API. YAML edit spec:
  note: "x"
  instrument_info:
    "a = 1\n[root]\nrate": "9"
loads cleanly via load_edit_spec (double-quoted key with \n is valid YAML; key never validated), and edit_config_text(..., add_keys=True) emits the key across multiple lines so a fresh `[root]` / `rate = 9` stanza appears in the patched config. parse_config() of the result returns root.rate == '9' (original was 512). This is precisely the acquisition-parameter corruption the docstring (lines 18-20) promises is impossible. I also confirmed a key containing '=' (e.g. "foo = bar": "9") silently mis-writes to `foo = bar = 9`, which parse_config reads as key 'foo' -> value 'bar = 9' — corrupted edit.

The test suite makes the gap unambiguous: tests test_block_scalar_value_injection_rejected (line 433) and test_escaped_newline_value_rejected (line 440) assert that the IDENTICAL [root]/[matrix] newline injection is rejected on the VALUE side (and note/author/source_label are likewise hardened), but there is no key-validation test and the code leaves keys unchecked. The author clearly modeled this exact threat for every interpolated text source except the map key.

Severity held at major (not blocker): it does not affect every valid run or crash the default path. All injection variants route through the add-key branch, so they require the operator to pass --add-keys AND write a malformed/malicious key into their own YAML (same-user CLI, not an untrusted-network surface) — most plausibly an accidental '=' in a key name producing silent config corruption, or a deliberate newline defeating the documented unaddressability invariant. It silently corrupts real output (the patched .p config / acquisition parameters) and breaks a hard documented guarantee, with a trivial fix (apply the same _check_value control-char/ASCII/';' checks plus an '=[]' blacklist to keys before storing). That is squarely major.

confirmed.title: Provenance comment generator and the 'when' field are not control-char-sanitized, unlike the banner (defense-in-depth gap / latent injection)
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified the code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. The structural claim is correct: `_build_banner` (lines 451-465) deliberately runs author/note/source_label through `_one_line()` at line 455, with an explicit comment (451-454) that a stray newline must not "spill past the ';' prefix and become active config." But `_one_line` is NEVER applied to `when` (interpolated raw at banner line 459 and provenance line 351), and `provenance()` (lines 350-357) interpolates spec.author, spec.note, ch.old, ch.new, ch.where, ch.key with no sanitization at all. So the banner hardening and the provenance/when path genuinely diverge.

I ran the injection. The finding's LITERAL reproduction is OVERSTATED/WRONG: for the cited config (`[instrument_info]\nsn=479\n[root]\nrate=512`), `edit_config_text(..., note='ok\\r\\n[root]\\r\\nrate=7', when='W')` parses to `root.rate == '512'`, NOT '7', because the real later `[root]` block clobbers the injected one (parse_config, p_file.py:216 splits on \r/\n; last assignment wins). The injection only takes effect when the injected key is not subsequently overridden — e.g. with config `[instrument_info]\nsn=479\n` I get `root == {'rate': '7)', 'sn': '480'}` (note: '7)', the template's trailing paren) via the note->provenance path, and a `when`-based injection likewise lands a `root.rate`. So the underlying vulnerability is real, but the exact PoC and value in the writeup are inaccurate.

Exploitability on this branch is nil on every shipped path, which the finding concedes and I confirmed: the only CLI entry `_cmd_patch_config` (cli.py:272-293) builds `when` via `datetime.now().strftime(...)` (machine-controlled, line 279) and always obtains `spec` from `load_edit_spec`, which rejects control chars in note (lines 185-186) and author (line 191). edit_config_text/patch_files/EditSpec are module-level functions importable from config_patch but are NOT re-exported via rsi/__init__.py and have no live caller passing attacker data. Test gap is real: `test_banner_source_label_newline_sanitized` (test_config_patch.py:463) covers source_label only; nothing exercises `when` or the provenance fields.

Net: a genuine latent defense-in-depth inconsistency (the banner was hardened; provenance and the `when` field were missed) with a correct, trivial suggested fix, but no corruption of any real output on the default/shipped path. real=true; severity correctly minor (nit would also be defensible given it is purely latent, but the divergence from an explicitly-hardened sibling sink keeps it at minor). Reproduction in the writeup should be corrected before it is trusted verbatim.

confirmed.title: rsi-tpw patch-config surfaces an uncaught ruamel YAMLError traceback on malformed edit-spec YAML
confirmed.reasoning: Verified and reproduced on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. _cmd_patch_config (cli.py:280-293) wraps load_edit_spec+patch_files in try/except (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError) -> print 'Error: ...' + sys.exit(1). load_edit_spec (config_patch.py:168-169) calls `data = yaml.load(fh)` with ruamel (typ='rt') and NO try/except around the parse, despite a docstring claiming it raises ValueError 'on any malformed input.' I confirmed ruamel's exception hierarchy: ParserError/ScannerError subclass only YAMLError -> Exception -> BaseException; YAMLError is NOT a subclass of ValueError or OSError. So a YAML syntax error escapes the handler. Reproduced end-to-end with the real CLI: `rsi-tpw patch-config tests/data/SN479_0006.p --edits badsyntax.yaml --out ...` (edits content 'note: \"x\"\\ninstrument_info: {bad yaml\\n') printed a full ruamel.yaml.parser.ParserError traceback (cli.py:281 -> config_patch.py:169 -> ruamel) rather than a friendly 'Error: ...'. PR #78 (commit 1a78fca) did not modify cli.py or config_patch.py (verified via git log main..HEAD), so the path is unchanged on this branch. tests/test_config_patch.py covers many semantic-validation errors via load_edit_spec but none feed syntactically-invalid YAML, confirming the test gap. The defect is genuine but the impact is cosmetic: this is the auxiliary patch-config config-editing subcommand, not the primary epsilon/chi processing path; the command still exits non-zero (1), so no data corruption, no silent loss, and no wrong scientific result -- only an ugly traceback instead of a clean message on a common-but-recoverable user input error. Downgrading minor -> nit on that basis. Suggested fix is correct: wrap yaml.load() in try/except ruamel.yaml.YAMLError and re-raise as ValueError(f'{path.name}: invalid YAML: {e}'), or add YAMLError to the except tuple in _cmd_patch_config.

confirmed.title: ct_align discards whole profile segment on a single isolated NaN; falls back to no-alignment when every profile has a bad sample
confirmed.reasoning: The mechanism is real and I reproduced it. ct_align.py:83-84 `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue` discards the ENTIRE profile segment on a single non-finite sample. A single input NaN does propagate widely (449/899 samples post-IIR; and np.mean(np.diff) NaNs the whole segment regardless), so the segment cannot be salvaged by the current code. There is genuinely no upstream NaN repair: perturb/pipeline.py:1049-1051 passes raw pf.channels[T_name]/[C_name] straight in (the finding cited rsi/pipeline.py:1049 but the real call site is perturb/pipeline.py:1049 — minor mis-cite, claim holds). Reproduction: 3 profiles each with one isolated NaN conductivity sample -> ct_align returns lag=0.0 and C unchanged (identity), while the clean consensus lag is 0.046875 s. The M3 regression test (test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:114-139) only exercises one NaN profile co-existing with a clean profile, which the weighted median handles correctly (I confirmed 1 bad among 4 clean recovers 0.046875 s) — so the all-profiles-bad over-discard mode is untested. HOWEVER the finding is materially overstated, which lowers severity from major to minor: (1) Its central justification — that the pre-fix code 'would NOT have wiped the whole file' — is FALSE. I reconstructed and ran the pre-fix ct_align: in the exact all-NaN-profiles scenario it produced lag = -5.0 s, the maximally-wrong -max_lag shift (a ~320-sample corruption of salinity/density/N2). PR #78's fallback to lag=0.0 (no shift) is therefore a STRICT IMPROVEMENT over the pre-fix behavior, not a regression. (2) The realistic worst case is loss of CT time-alignment (output = unaligned conductivity), not 'silent corruption' — and only in the narrow regime where every profile in a file carries a transient bad sample; any single clean profile recovers the correct lag. (3) The MATLAB faithfulness claim ('the MATLAB reference would NOT have wiped the whole file') is unverifiable: Code/CT_align.m is not present in the repo. Net: a genuine edge-case refinement opportunity (interpolate/mask the few bad samples rather than discarding the segment, and add an all-bad-profiles test), but the impact is a degraded-not-corrupted CT product in a rare case, and PR #78 already made this path safer than the code it replaced. Minor, not major.

confirmed.title: detect_bottom_crash: np.errstate(invalid="ignore") does not suppress the "All-NaN slice encountered" RuntimeWarning from np.nanmax, so legitimate all-NaN depth segments leak warning noise
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code. bottom.py:89-90 reads `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore"): max_depth = np.nanmax(depth)`. The core technical claim is correct: np.errstate governs only IEEE FP flags (invalid/divide/over/under), NOT numpy's _methods Python-level RuntimeWarning. I confirmed in isolation on numpy 2.4.3 that `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore"): np.nanmax(np.full(5, np.nan))` still emits `RuntimeWarning: All-NaN slice encountered`. I then ran the actual test (tests/test_processing_bottom.py::test_all_nan_depth_returns_none) under `-W default -rw`: it passes but emits exactly `bottom.py:90: RuntimeWarning: All-NaN slice encountered`. So the errstate wrapper provably fails to suppress the warning it was evidently added to silence. Practical impact is limited to benign log noise: line 93 (`if not np.isfinite(max_depth) ...: return None`) correctly handles the resulting NaN, so there is no crash and no corrupted output. This is genuinely cosmetic/warning-hygiene, consistent with the project's own standard (it has dedicated no-warning-leak tests like test_all_nan_temperature_no_empty_slice_warning and test_all_nan_window_emits_no_warning), so nit is the correct severity. One minor overstatement in the evidence: the finding says the comment "implies errstate suppresses it," but the inline comment (lines 91-92) only explains the bail-out and never claims errstate suppression — there is no comment on line 89 at all. That overstatement does not change the verdict: the defect (ineffective warning suppression -> leaked RuntimeWarning) is real on this branch. Not fixed by PR #78 (the cited line is unchanged).

confirmed.title: despike() raises scipy ValueError on length-1/length-2 sections; reachable via process_l2_chi temperature despike
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified the crash and reachability against current branch code.

CRASH CONFIRMED (runtime): despike(np.zeros(L), 512.0) raises "ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6." for L=1 and L=2; L>=3 succeeds. The candidate's mechanism is exact: despike.py:98 pad_len = min(length, 2*int(fs/smooth)); for length=2, pad_len=2, so dv_padded at lines 99-105 has 2*2+2 = 6 samples. The 1st-order Butterworth at line 109 (len(a)=len(b)=2) makes scipy.signal.filtfilt at line 110 use default padlen 3*max(len(a),len(b)) = 6, and filtfilt requires len(x) > padlen strictly. 6 is not > 6, so it raises. (length=1 -> padded 3 also fails.) I re-derived pad_len/padded_len for L=1..7 and they match.

REACHABILITY CONFIRMED (production chi path): The candidate's call-site name is slightly off (the function is process_l2_chi, not compute_l2_chi) but the line is right: chi/l2_chi.py:105 calls despike(temp_fast[ci, mask], fs, thresh=t_thresh, smooth=t_smooth, N=N_t) per (section, channel), and the branch is entered whenever t_thresh is finite (l2_chi.py:98). The default despike_T = [10.0, 0.5, 0.04] (L2ChiParams, l2_chi.py:54-56) has finite thresh, so the despike branch runs by default. The per-section mask = l2.section_number == sec_id comes, in production (rsi/chi_io.py:151,154), from process_l2(l1, l2_params) with l2_params.profile_min_duration=0.0 (chi_io.py:116). _select_sections (scor160/l2.py:248-252) then accepts ANY contiguous run satisfying speed>=0.05 and pres>=0.0 with duration>=0.0 — including a 1-2 sample run. I reproduced this: _select_sections on a speed series with an isolated 2-sample run above 0.05 m/s yields one section of length 2, which would be passed straight to despike and crash chi L2 for that profile.

NOT FIXED BY PR #78: PR #78 touched chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — neither despike.py nor l2_chi.py. The current despike.py has no padlen guard.

SEVERITY: Kept at minor. This is a genuine hard crash but an edge case: it requires a degenerate 1-2 sample contiguous good-speed run WITHIN a profile that already passed the upstream 7 s profile-extraction (prepare_profiles uses min_duration=7.0). On typical clean vertical VMP descents speed stays well above 0.05 m/s, so such isolated micro-sections are atypical; they arise mainly from speed jitter crossing the 0.05 threshold near profile boundaries. It does not corrupt every valid run (not a blocker) and is not silent corruption of normal output (not major) — it is an off-nominal/edge-case crash, i.e. minor. The MATLAB port-faithfulness framing (odas/despike.m using MATLAB filtfilt's padlen=3*(max(len)-1)=3) is plausible but secondary; the actionable defect is the crash, and the suggested fix (explicit padlen or a short-input guard) is correct.

confirmed.title: A single NaN sample silently disables ALL spike detection for the whole array (real spikes pass through into chi/epsilon)
confirmed.reasoning: The mechanism is real and I reproduced it exactly: despike.py:110/114 use scipy filtfilt, which propagates a single NaN across the whole array, so dv_hp/dv_lp become all-NaN, ratio = dv_hp/dv_lp (line 118) is all-NaN, and `ratio > thresh` (line 119) is all-False. My runtime test (10000-sample signal, real 50x spike at idx 3000, one NaN at idx 7000) returned spikes=0, passes=0, frac=0.0, and y[3000]=50.0 (spike NOT removed) — matching the finding's claim verbatim. However, the MAJOR severity is overstated because the finding's headline impact path is NOT reachable on this branch. (1) The FP07 temperature channel that feeds despike at chi/l2_chi.py:105 is produced by convert_therm (channels.py:89-129), which CLIPS Z to [-0.6,0.6] (line 119) → R_ratio always in [0.25,4.0] → log_R always finite → temperature output always finite. An FP07 dropout/saturation therefore CANNOT emit NaN, so the specific 'FP07 NaN biases chi' scenario does not occur. convert_shear (channels.py:132-145) is purely linear and likewise NaN-free; the only NaN-emitting converter is convert_jac_c (JAC conductivity, line 238), which does not feed the temperature/shear despike. (2) Port-faithful: ODAS despike.m (lines 184,192) uses the identical filtfilt with no NaN guard; the finding's parenthetical that 'ODAS upstream removes/interpolates NaNs before despiking' is an unsubstantiated assertion about an unspecified upstream stage, not despike.m. (3) The behavior is a KNOWN, codified design point: tests/test_scor160_despike.py:30-39 (test_nan_input_reports_zero_fraction) explicitly asserts n_passes==0 with docstring 'no spikes detectable through the NaN', and the round-1 fix only addressed fraction inflation. So this is a genuine LATENT robustness gap (if NaN ever entered a despiked channel — e.g. a future sensor or gappy external ATOMIX .nc shear — spikes would silently survive with no warning), worth a defensive NaN-guard, but it is not silent corruption on any valid run of the real pipeline. Real=true, corrected severity nit (latent/defensive), not major.

confirmed.title: despike.py both-empty replacement falls back to 0.0 instead of MATLAB's NaN
confirmed.reasoning:
The code divergence is real and I confirmed it. At despike.py:163-166 the both-empty branch sets replacement=0.0, whereas the ODAS reference (odas/despike.m:235-243) computes sum(points)/length(points) which for empty points is 0/0=NaN, giving (NaN+NaN)/2=NaN. I also confirmed the related one-sided divergence: in MATLAB (value+NaN)/2=NaN, so MATLAB does NOT one-side-fall-back, while the port at lines 159-162 does. I verified the both-empty branch is logically reachable: when an entire (sub)span is marked bad with no good points in the R=256 neighborhood on either side, before_vals and after_vals are both empty and the port writes 0.0 where MATLAB writes NaN.

However the finding is OVERSTATED on practical impact and its central claim is inaccurate for the real pipeline. (1) Reachability is pathological: the both-empty branch only triggers when a contiguous all-bad span exceeds the full R-neighborhood (~512 samples at fs=512) with zero valid points — a near-saturated channel, not nominal microstructure data; my realistic dense-spike tests did not hit it, only an all-bad construction did. (2) The QC that governs rejection is fraction-based, not fill-value-based: at l4.py:735 flags[despike_fraction > despike_fraction_limit] += 2 with DEFAULT_DESPIKE_FRACTION_LIMIT=0.05 (l4.py:72). Any window saturated enough to reach the both-empty branch has a despike fraction far above 5% and is already QC-flagged regardless of whether the fill is 0.0 or NaN, so the claim that 0.0 "silently passes through all NaN-based rejection" while NaN would be "detectable" does not hold. (3) A NaN fill would propagate through the spectral FFT and poison the entire window's estimate — arguably worse than a benign 0.0 that the fraction-based QC then rejects. (4) The port is validated against ODAS reference L2 data (l2.py:88-92 documents that the reference contains the same exactly-constant replacement values), which it would fail if MATLAB routinely emitted NaN in the far-more-common one-sided edge case. No test asserts NaN behavior here. PR #78 does not touch despike.py. Net: a genuine but latent port-faithfulness divergence with no demonstrated effect on any real result on this branch, independently caught by DESPIKE_FRACTION QC; this is a nit, not a minor silent-corruption defect.

confirmed.title: K_rho N2_min floor (1e-9 s^-2) only prevents division-by-zero, not unphysical inflation; near-floor windows emit absurd Osborn diffusivities
confirmed.reasoning: Factual claims verified against current branch code. mixing.py:486 emits K_rho = gamma_osborn * epsilon / N2 (no upper clamp) whenever strat_ok & eps_ok, where strat_ok = (N2_min <= N2) at line 475 with DEFAULT_N2_MIN=1e-9 (line 57). The cited arithmetic is correct: eps=1e-7, N2=2e-9 yields K_rho=10 m^2/s, ~4-5 orders above realistic ocean diapycnal diffusivity (1e-5 to 1e-3 m^2/s). Crucially, the scenario is physically realizable in THIS pipeline: N2 is produced by _stable_window (mixing.py:293-319) via gsw.Nsquared between Thorpe-sorted (statically stable) half-means, so N2 is guaranteed >=0 and lands arbitrarily close to zero in well-mixed turbulent layers (active surface/bottom mixed layers, eps~1e-7) -- exactly the near-floor regime described. The plot filter at rsi/mixing_look.py:323 (valid = isfinite(vals) & (vals>0)) would indeed pass such divergent values through. No upper sanity clamp exists anywhere. PR #78 does not touch mixing.py (git diff main...HEAD: only chi.py, bottom.py, ct_align.py, profile.py), so it is neither fixed nor a duplicate of a PR #78 item. Severity stays at the reporter's 'nit': K_rho is a derived diagnostic, not a primary result -- epsilon and chi (the primary products) are unaffected; output is finite and arithmetically correct (no crash, NaN/Inf, or corruption of primary results); the divergence is explicitly documented as a known, configurable-floor limitation in the module docstring and comment (line 56); and where exactly to floor weak stratification is a legitimate modeling judgment (raising the floor to 1e-7 would discard real weakly-stratified estimates). A genuine latent shortcoming worth noting, but correctly rated nit -- it is an interpretation/cosmetic concern, not a computation defect.

confirmed.title: rsi run_pipeline mixing quantities use the unfiltered chi_final geometric mean (no fom/K_max_ratio QC), unlike the epsilon path that co-drives Gamma/K_rho
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. The core observation holds: `_compute_chi_final` (l4_chi.py:286-294) computes `chi_final[j] = exp(mean(log(chi[good,j])))` over every probe with finite chi>0 with NO filtering on per-window `fom` or `K_max_ratio`, and pipeline.py:466 feeds this unfiltered `chi_final` straight into `mixing_coefficients`, which produces the WRITTEN NetCDF outputs K_T (=chi/(2 dTdz^2), pipeline.py:501-509) and Gamma (=N2*chi/(2*eps*dTdz^2), pipeline.py:513). The epsilon co-driving Gamma/K_rho IS QC-filtered: `_compute_epsi_final` (l4.py:766-779) means only probes with `flags==0`, and `_compute_flags` (l4.py:731) sets `flags[fom > fom_limit] += 1` per-probe regardless of probe count, with fallback to all-finite only if none survive. So a genuine within-pipeline asymmetry exists (epsilon fom-filtered, chi not), affecting a written diagnostic — the defect is real.

BUT the finding is materially overstated in its framing and suggested fix. (1) It claims the bias occurs "in the rsi path ONLY" because perturb's `mk_chi_mean` does CI-based probe removal. Reading chi_combine.py:163-188, `mk_chi_mean` does INTER-PROBE CI outlier rejection, NOT fom/K_max_ratio filtering — a different criterion that would not catch the high-fom/low-K_max_ratio probe the finding's own suggested fix targets. (2) That CI removal only runs with `n_probes >= 3` (line 163) and `finite_count > 2` (line 178). The default ARCTERX VMP-250 SN479 (verified via PFile: channels T1, T2, T1_dT1, T2_dT2) has exactly 2 thermistors, so `n_gradt=2` and `mk_chi_mean`'s removal is a no-op — the perturb path geometric-means both probes unfiltered, IDENTICALLY to the rsi path. The "rsi path only" claim is false on the real default config. (3) No chi path anywhere applies fom/K_max_ratio thresholds (confirmed by grepping chi/*.py and processing/chi_combine.py — fom/K_max_ratio are only computed and stored, never thresholded), so the suggested fix is new behavior in both paths, not "mirroring" mk_chi_mean.

Severity stays minor: the impact is a possible bias on secondary derived mixing diagnostics (K_T/Gamma/K_rho) only when a thermistor window has a poor spectral fit, never on the primary chi/epsilon products; chi_final matches the documented combined product and the perturb path's actual behavior on default 2-probe data. Real but bounded edge-case bias, with an overstated rsi-vs-perturb contrast.

confirmed.title: _iterative_fit returns a chi computed against a one-iteration-stale kB on convergence break
confirmed.reasoning: Mechanism verified in current code on this branch. In _iterative_fit (chi.py:544-607), each iteration first calls _mle_find_kB to get kB_best (l.546), then on iteration>0 tests convergence `abs(kB_best - kB_prev)/kB_prev < 0.01` and breaks (l.561-562) BEFORE the recompute block (l.565-596) that updates chi_obs from the current kB_best. chi_obs depends on kB through k_l_new = max(K[1], 3*k_star), k_star = 0.04*kB*sqrt(KAPPA_T/nu) (l.566-567) and through correction = _variance_correction(kB_best, ...) (l.588-589). So when the break fires, the returned kB_best/epsilon (l.600) use the just-found kB, while the returned chi = chi_obs (l.606) was last assigned in the prior iteration using kB_prev, and spec_batch = grad_func(K, kB_best, chi) (l.607) mixes the fresh kB with the stale chi. The defect is genuinely present and not fixed by PR #78 (the recompute block is unchanged). However, the impact is inherently bounded by the convergence test itself: the two kB values differ by <1% by construction. I empirically confirmed the chi consequence is tiny: a +0.9% kB step changes _variance_correction by only 0.06%-0.42% across kB in [5,30] and leaves k_l essentially unchanged (1.00->1.00, or 1.23->1.24 at kB=30). So the actual chi inconsistency is well under 1% — even smaller than the finding's stated <1-2%. No silent corruption, no wrong primary result, no crash; all returned quantities are mutually consistent to within the iteration's own convergence tolerance. This is a real but minimal numerical-consistency imperfection, correctly classified as a nit. Severity confirmed at nit (lowest meaningful), not promoted.

confirmed.title: _bilinear_correction raises ValueError for small config-derived diff_gain (cutoff >= 1)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current code on fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. chi.py:644 is `butter(1, 1/(2*np.pi*diff_gain*fs/2))`. The math holds: with fs=512, a diff_gain <= ~6.2e-4 makes the normalized cutoff Wn >= 1, and scipy's butter raises `ValueError: Digital filter critical frequencies must be 0 < Wn < 1`. Confirmed empirically: diff_gain=1e-4 -> Wn=6.22 (raises); diff_gain=6.2e-4 -> Wn=1.003 (raises); the default 0.94 -> Wn=0.0007 (fine). The call site is l3_chi.py:296 (`bl_corrections = [_bilinear_correction(F_const, dg, fs) for dg in diff_gains]`) with no try/except, so an out-of-range value would abort L3 chi for the cast rather than degrade gracefully. diff_gain is sourced from the instrument config (adapter.py:164, chi_io.py:741, viewer_base.py:433), defaulting to 0.94 — the standard FP07 differentiator gain, which is O(1). Triggering this requires a diff_gain ~1500x smaller than the standard value, which no valid instrument produces; the finding itself concedes trusted-RSI likelihood is low. The new patch-config path (config_patch.py) does only string-level/control-char validation with no semantic range check on diff_gain, so a user could in principle patch a pathological value, but only via a deliberately nonsensical edit. Crucially, this is a faithful port of ODAS get_scalar_spectra_odas.m:265 (`[b,a] = butter(1, 1/(2*pi*diff_gain(k)*fs/2))`), which is identically unguarded — MATLAB's butter would error the same way, and ODAS's val_diff_gain (line 124) only rejects negatives, not this lower bound. So it is not a port deviation or regression. Not touched by PR #78 (4 unrelated major fixes). It is a genuine latent robustness gap reachable only under corrupt/adversarial config, not silent corruption or a default-path crash, so the original self-assigned "nit" severity is honest and correct.

confirmed.title: longitude x-axis passthrough is not dateline-safe: a track crossing +/-180 is split/reordered (latitude part of claim is spurious)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors (PR #78 touched chi/profile/ct_align/bottom, NOT xaxis.py — this code is untouched). The core defect is real: xaxis.compute() lines 324-327 return raw degree values verbatim (x=np.asarray(lon, float)) with no unwrapping, while the distance/along_line/signed_distance methods were all hardened for the dateline via _circmean_deg (line 72) and _wrap_deg (line 67), and carry dedicated regression tests (tests/test_perturb_plot_xaxis.py:27,220,229,241). The longitude passthrough has no equivalent protection. The downstream consumption chain in the evidence checks out exactly: profiles.py:208 sorts columns by raw x via finite[np.argsort(x[finite])], and layout.column_clusters() (layout.py:170) inserts a cluster break wherever a gap exceeds gap_factor*median_spacing. So a continuous track lon=[179,179.5,-179.5,-179] (physically ~1 deg wide) argsorts to [-179.5,-179,179,179.5], reordering casts and producing a spurious ~358-deg gap that splits the section into two far-apart clusters — silently misregistering the section plot. 'longitude' is a valid user-selectable method (METHODS, line 36). However, severity is correctly minor, not higher, for three reasons: (1) it only triggers when the user explicitly selects the longitude x-axis AND the survey track physically crosses +/-180 — an off-nominal edge case; (2) it affects only a secondary plotting/visualization output, never the primary epsilon/chi scientific result, so no scientific corruption; (3) the project's actual ARCTERX/Saipan data (~145E) never crosses the dateline, as the finding itself concedes ('Low urgency'). One overstatement in the finding: the title and evidence implicate 'latitude' too, but latitude is bounded to [-90,90] and never wraps at +/-180, so the latitude passthrough is not affected — only longitude has the dateline problem. Net: a genuine latent edge-case defect in plotting code, real but minor.

confirmed.title: eps_chi profile realignment over-reports 'eps slots have no chi' by counting validly-matched but all-NaN chi columns as unmatched
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. At eps_chi.py:323, `n_unmatched = int(np.sum(~np.isfinite(aligned).any(axis=0)))` counts any column of `aligned` that has no finite value. Because `aligned` is filled at line 320 (`aligned[:, k] = chi[:, j]`) inside the `if abs(eps_secs[k] - s) <= 5` time-match branch, a chi column that WAS time-matched but is entirely NaN over depth still produces an all-NaN aligned column and is counted as 'no chi'. The mechanism is real and the cited evidence is accurate. All-NaN-but-time-valid chi columns are a genuine outcome: the legacy loader `_load_chi_legacy_per_profile` (lines 75-91) initializes `chi` to all-NaN and only fills bins where `np.isfinite(c)` and the digitize index is in range, while always assigning a valid `times[j]`; a profile QC-dropped at every depth (or an FP07 cast with no finite chi) yields exactly such a column. So the printed diagnostic can overstate the count of genuinely time-unmatched eps slots.\n\nHowever, impact is minimal and the original severity of 'nit' is correct. (1) `n_unmatched` is used ONLY in the print() at lines 324-328 (confirmed by grep: the sole reference outside line 323). It does not affect `chi`, `chi_qc`, the pcolor plot, gamma=chi/eps, or any saved NetCDF/figure output. (2) The block is off the common path: lines 300-305 only trigger realignment when chi/eps profile counts differ or times misalign by >5s; the bin-for-bin matched case (noted as 'the common matched-grid case' at line 120) skips it entirely. (3) The message text 'eps slots have no chi' is arguably accurate for an all-NaN column — that slot truly has no plottable chi — so it is borderline whether this is 'wrong' versus merely ambiguously worded. This is a cosmetic diagnostic-accuracy issue in a plotting script that could mildly mislead a user reading the console message while debugging a chi shortfall. Not in scope of PR #78 (which touched chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py), so not fixed by it and not a duplicate. Real but a nit.

confirmed.title: L4 NaN-speed window yields wrong-but-finite epsilon: K_AA derived from un-floored speed (max(NaN,0.05)=NaN)
confirmed.reasoning: The code reading is accurate. l4.py:175 reads the un-floored l3.pspd_rel[j]; l4.py:180 computes K_AA = 0.9*f_AA/max(W,0.05); and max(np.nan,0.05)=nan (verified). l3.py:212 floors W only for the wavenumber axis while l3.py:185-189 deliberately stores the un-floored mean (NaN for an all-NaN window, comment #59), so a NaN speed reaches L4 and makes K_AA=NaN. That part is genuine and currently inconsistent. However, the finding's PRIMARY impact claim is overstated/wrong. It asserts n_var=len(K) (full-spectrum over-integration) producing a biased-high finite epsilon. I traced _variance_method and ran faithful all-NaN-window reconstructions. Although line 480 (index_limit=searchsorted(K,NaN)=len(K)) only sets the polyfit window upper bound, the FINAL integration count n_var (line 516) is derived from 10**K_limit_log, and line 510's three-argument min(K_limit_log, log10(K_95), log10(K_AA)) keeps the NaN log10(K_AA) as the LAST argument so the finite K_95 term wins and K_limit_log stays finite. Thus n_var is bounded by K_95, NEVER len(K). In a faithful all-NaN window (K axis built with floored W=0.05 per l3.py:212, Psi(k)=Psi(f)*0.05) the buggy K_AA=NaN and the suggested-fix floored K_AA=1764 cpm give IDENTICAL epsilon/K_max/method/fom for Nasmyth-shaped spectra; a measurable divergence (up to ~21%) appears only at very high epsilon (~1.6e-6) where K_95 exceeds the available wavenumber range and K_AA would be the binding limit, and even there it is a different finite value, not the claimed entire-spectrum over-integration. Practical reachability is also marginal: the standard pipeline floors speed at speed_min (profile.py:154), so NaN speed essentially never occurs on valid/benchmark runs, and test_scor160_l3.py:134 confirms the all-NaN case was already handled at L3 (#59). Net: a genuine latent inconsistency worth the one-line defensive floor, but it does not cause silent corruption of real output and the described impact does not hold. Downgrade minor to nit; not fixed by PR #78 (none of the 4 fixes touch l4.py K_AA).

confirmed.title: compare_l2 raises ValueError on empty section arrays (.max() on zero-size)
confirmed.reasoning: Code-level claim is correct and empirically confirmed. compare.py:51-52 call int(ref_sec.max()) and int(comp_sec.max()) unconditionally, before the both_in.any() guard at line 59. np.ndarray.max() on a zero-size array raises ValueError('zero-size array to reduction operation maximum which has no identity'). I built an empty L2Data and called compare_l2(empty, empty); it crashed with exactly that ValueError. The inconsistency is real too: the sibling functions compare_l3 (compare.py:186-187) and compare_l4 (compare.py:294-295) both guard empty input with an early `if n_common == 0: return results`, and L3 even has a dedicated test_empty_data test (tests/test_scor160_compare.py:112) — but compare_l2 has neither guard nor empty-input test.\n\nDowngrading minor -> nit because practical impact on this branch is near-zero. The crash requires a genuinely zero-length array. _select_sections (l2.py:226-230) returns np.zeros(n); when there are samples but NO qualifying sections it returns an all-zeros array (size n>0), and .max() on all-zeros returns 0.0 with no crash (verified). So the evidence's stated 'no sections selected' scenario does NOT trigger the bug — only a literally empty (n_time==0) input does. The sole production caller is the scor160 CLI compare subcommand (cli.py:109), which feeds l2_comp=process_l2(l1,...) and l2_ref from read_atomix(path); both derive from real ATOMIX benchmark NetCDF files that always contain samples, so neither section_number is ever zero-length on that path. No current code path or test passes an empty L1 into compare_l2. PR #78 does not touch this file. It is a genuine but latent robustness gap on a public-yet-internally-unexercised function, not a defect affecting any real run, hence nit rather than minor.

confirmed.title: L4Data.fom docstring mislabels the benchmark FOM as the obs/Nasmyth variance ratio
confirmed.reasoning: Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. io.py:198 comments the L4Data.fom field as 'figure of merit (obs/Nasmyth variance ratio)'. But _read_l4 (io.py:666) populates that field directly from the benchmark NetCDF's FOM variable. Two independent in-repo comments confirm that the benchmark/reference FOM is NOT the variance ratio but the Lueck (2022) MAD-normalized statistic: compare.py:350-352 states 'The benchmark reference FOM is the MAD-based Lueck (2022) FM statistic' and accordingly pairs computed.FM against reference.fom (compare.py:354-357); l4.py:213-216 likewise notes the fom_limit default was written for 'the MAD-based FM statistic ... the ATOMIX convention'. The genuine variance-ratio quantity is the separate computed.fom, defined in l4.py:413 as fom_val = obs_var/nas_var, while the MAD statistic is computed.FM (l4.py:427-434). So the comment mislabels the reference field as the wrong sibling quantity — especially confusing because L4Data also carries a real variance-ratio fom on the computed side. The defect is real and holds on this branch. PR #78's fix commit (1a78fca) touches only chi.py, bottom.py, ct_align.py, profile.py — not io.py — so it is unaffected. Severity 'nit' is correct: it is a comment on a dataclass field with no runtime effect; the actual data flow in compare.py is correct. I could not independently re-read the NetCDF FOM long_name attribute (no ATOMIX benchmark .nc exists on this filesystem), but two independent in-code comments corroborate the candidate's reading, so confidence is high.

confirmed.title: Method-1 epsilon time reference is corrupted by tz-offset stripping, silently NaN-ing all chi for non-UTC instruments
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code; the defect genuinely holds. (1) chi_io.py:382-390 strips the tz offset with `start = np.datetime64(re.sub(r"(Z|[+-]\d{2}:?\d{2})$", "", start_str))`, and the comment on line 386 ("numpy.datetime64 doesn't accept tz suffix") is factually wrong: I reproduced that `np.datetime64('2025-01-15T03:04:05+10:00')` returns 2025-01-14T17:04:05 (correctly UTC-normalized, only a UserWarning), whereas stripping yields the naive local time, a 36000 s error for +10:00. (2) start_str genuinely carries an offset: pf.start_time is built with a real tzinfo from the header timezone_min word (p_file.py:326-344) and is serialized via .isoformat() into both the start_time attr and the t units "seconds since <isoformat>" (dissipation.py:296-297, profile.py:309/321). (3) load_epsilon_dataset opens with xr.open_dataset defaults (chi_io.py:865), so decode_cf decodes the t coord to datetime64 — I round-tripped a synthetic +10:00 epsilon NetCDF through this branch's write/read code and the recovered seconds came out [-36000,-35990,-35980] instead of [0,10,20]. (4) Downstream, process_l4_chi_epsilon (l4_chi.py:164-176) compares these corrupted epsi_times against genuine seconds-since-start chi_times with max_dt = median(diff) = 10 s, so every window's |Δt| ~= 36000 s >> max_dt returns None -> Method-1 chi all-NaN. (5) Test gap confirmed: every _epsilon_ds_to_l4data test in tests/test_chi.py uses a raw float t coord (e.g. np.arange(n, dtype=float)) with no CF units, so xarray never decodes it and the datetime64 tz-strip branch (lines 383-390) is entirely untested. Scope/severity: this is conditional, not a blocker — the bundled ARCTERX campaign file is +00:00 (I checked: pf.start_time.utcoffset() == 0), so on the only real data in the repo, and on any UTC/zero-offset deployment, the strip is harmless; Method-2 chi is also unaffected. But for a genuine non-UTC deployment it silently NaN-s all Method-1 chi output, which is silent loss of real output -> major is the correct severity.

confirmed.title: _epsilon_ds_to_l4data filters the multi-probe epsilon mean on `fom` (obs/Nasmyth ratio) at the 1.15 threshold meant for the `FM` statistic, which it never reads
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. (1) chi_io.py:31 defines `_EPS_FOM_LIMIT = 1.15`; line 353 reads `fom_arr = _probe_var("fom")` and line 365 filters `bad = ~np.isfinite(fom_arr) | (fom_arr > _EPS_FOM_LIMIT)` to exclude probes before the cross-probe epsilon mean. (2) dissipation.py:455-481 writes BOTH `fom` (long_name 'figure of merit (observed/Nasmyth variance ratio)', good ~1.0; its own comment says 'NOT the MAD-based ATOMIX/Rockland FM statistic') and `FM` (Lueck 2022, comment 'Good fits approach 0; ATOMIX recommends rejecting FM > ~1.15'). So 1.15 is the FM threshold, applied here to the wrong variable. (3) The canonical L4 code confirms this: l4.py:216 sets `fom_for_flags = FM if num_ffts > 0 else fom` with the comment 'the ATOMIX convention the fom_limit default of 1.15 was written for', and _compute_flags (l4.py:731) applies `> fom_limit` to FM. chi_io does the inverse. (4) Reachability: this branch is the `n_sh > 1` path (lines 358-378), reached via `compute_chi_file --epsilon-dir` → _epsilon_ds_to_l4data. The rsi epsilon NetCDF from compute_diss_file carries `fom` and `FM` but NOT `epsilonMean` (grep confirms epsilonMean is written only by processing/epsilon_combine.py, never the rsi pipeline), so the `epsilonMean` short-circuit at line 356 is False and the fom-filter is live for any 2-probe instrument (VMP SN479 = sh1+sh2). The resulting epsi_final feeds process_l4_chi_epsilon directly (chi_io.py:170) and sets the Batchelor wavenumber for chi Method-1, so the mis-filter silently biases that chi output: good-but-energetic windows with fom in (1.15, 2.0] are wrongly dropped while genuinely bad probes with fom < 1.15 pass. (5) `FM` is present in the file and never read — confirmed: grep shows chi_io references only fom/mad, never FM. (6) Not fixed by PR #78 (touched chi/chi.py, processing/bottom.py, ct_align.py, rsi/profile.py — not chi_io.py). (7) Test gap confirmed: TestEpsilonDsToL4Data.test_falls_back_to_nanmean_when_mean_absent (tests/test_chi.py:843) exercises the multi-probe branch but supplies no `fom`, so the filter at line 364 (`if fom_arr is not None`) is skipped and the fom/FM behavior is untested. Severity held at minor (not major): chi Method-1 is the opt-in `--epsilon-dir` path, not the default chi route (default is Method-2 fit); an all-NaN-window fallback to the all-probe mean (lines 374-378) prevents catastrophic dropout; and the bias is bounded. This is silent corruption of a real but secondary, opt-in output, matching minor.

confirmed.title: load_epsilon_dataset concatenates a stale single-profile `_eps.nc` together with per-profile `_prof00N_eps.nc`, double-counting epsilon times
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code (chi_io.py:857-872), which is unchanged by PR #78 (git diff main...HEAD shows chi_io.py not in the changeset; the insert logic dates to PR #54). The finding is mechanically accurate: load_epsilon_dataset globs `{stem}_prof*_eps.nc` and then unconditionally does `if single.exists(): files.insert(0, single)` for `{stem}_eps.nc`, then when len>1 does `xr.concat(datasets, dim="time", combine_attrs="override")` with no overlap or duplicate-time guard. I confirmed by test that the prof* glob does NOT match the single-profile file, so both forms can end up in the concat list, and write_profile_results (helpers.py:473-477) does produce `{stem}_eps.nc` for 1 profile and `{stem}_prof00N_eps.nc` for many — mutually exclusive within one run. The `eps` CLI command (cli.py:353) writes flat into output_dir with no cleanup, so orphaned files of both forms can coexist if the same source is re-run into the same dir with a different profile count (1 vs many). When that happens, _epsilon_ds_to_l4data builds an epsilon time axis with overlapping/duplicate samples, which biases the nearest-neighbour epsilon driving Method-1 chi (process_l4_chi_epsilon) — genuine silent corruption. However the trigger is strictly off-nominal: it requires (a) the same file processed twice into the same directory, (b) profile detection disagreeing between the two runs, and (c) the Method-1 two-step `chi --epsilon-dir` workflow (the default `pipeline` computes epsilon in-memory and never exercises this path). It never fires on a clean run. The missing duplicate-time guard is a real robustness defect, so not-a-bug is wrong, but the narrow user-error trigger keeps it off the major tier. original 'minor' is well-calibrated; affirming minor.

confirmed.title: np.arange bin-edge construction produces a spurious extra trailing bin and reassigns the deepest boundary sample for non-binary-exact bin widths
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code. Lines 440-442 build the depth grid exactly as cited: d_min=np.floor(g_min/bw)*bw; d_max=np.ceil(g_max/bw)*bw; bin_edges=np.arange(d_min, d_max+bw, bw). Line 576 has the identical pattern for the time grid. I reproduced the off-by-one numerically: depths 0..7 m at bw=0.7 yield d_min=0.0, d_max=7.0, but np.arange emits edges [0,0.7,...,7.0,7.7] -> 11 bins instead of the intended 10 (expected int(round(7.0/0.7))=10). Binary-exact widths are all safe: bw=1.0/0.5/0.25/2.0/5.0/0.1 each gave nbins==expected. The deepest-sample claim also holds: _bin_indices (lines 44-46) clamps coords==bin_edges[-1] into the last bin; with the buggy 11-bin grid, np.digitize puts the depth==7.0 sample in index 10 = the spurious bin [7.0,7.7), so the intended deepest bin [6.3,7.0) (index 9) loses its boundary sample, whereas the correct 10-bin grid (edges[-1]==7.0) would clamp that sample into index 9. So the bug both adds a half-empty trailing bin AND misassigns the boundary sample. The test-gap claim is accurate: all bin_by_depth tests in tests/test_perturb_binning.py use binary-exact widths (100.0/5.0/20.0/1.0), and the only bin_width=0.1 tests (lines 442, 463) are bin_by_time tests asserting variable presence/absence, not bin count or edges. bin_width is fully user-configurable (config.py binning.width/diss_width/chi_width; cli.py line 232), so non-binary-exact widths are reachable. NOT fixed by PR #78 (which touched chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — not perturb/binning.py). Severity stays minor, not elevated: the defaults (1.0 m depth, 0.5 s time) are binary-exact so the default path is correct and unaffected; the impact when triggered is bounded (one extra mostly-empty bin plus one misassigned deepest boundary sample, no corruption of in-bin epsilon/chi values). It is a genuine latent off-nominal correctness bug, correctly classified minor with a sound integer-count fix.

confirmed.title: csd_matrix overlap<0 clamps to nfft//2 (50% overlap) where MATLAB clamps to 0 (no overlap)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified the divergence is real and accurately described. spectral.py:197-200 reads `if overlap < 0 or overlap > 0.9 * nfft: ... overlap = nfft // 2`, collapsing both clamp branches into nfft//2. The MATLAB reference odas/csd_matrix_odas.m:132-136 splits them: `if over_lap < 0, over_lap = 0; elseif over_lap > n_fft*0.9, over_lap = round(n_fft/2)`. So a negative overlap yields 0 (non-overlapping segments) in MATLAB but nfft//2 (50% overlap) in Python, which would change n_seg and the ensemble average. The same collapsed clamp exists in csd_matrix_batch at spectral.py:364. The >0.9*nfft branch already matches MATLAB (both -> nfft/2). PR #78 does not touch spectral.py (its fixes are in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py), so this is unchanged on this branch. However, reachability is nil in practice: I grepped every internal caller and all pass a positive overlap=nfft//2 (goodman.py:150,168,243; l3.py:148; l3_chi.py:141). The csd wrapper (spectral.py:127) forwards overlap but has zero internal callers (grep for csd( found none). overlap=None maps to nfft//2 before the clamp, so the clamp branch is only entered for an explicit negative overlap — an invalid input that no production path supplies. Both implementations silently coerce this nonsensical input rather than erroring; they simply coerce differently. Because it cannot affect any output the codebase actually computes and only concerns handling of an invalid external-API input, I downgrade minor -> nit: a genuine but purely latent port-faithfulness cosmetic divergence.

confirmed.title: csd_matrix/clean_shear_spec auto-transpose `shape[0] < shape[1]` flips legitimately wide matrices, unlike MATLAB's isrow-only force
