confirmed.reasoning: Code verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. spectral.py:180-181 transposes x when `x.shape[0] < x.shape[1]` (and 189-190 for y); goodman.py:138-141 does the same for accel/shear. The MATLAB references genuinely differ: csd_matrix_odas.m:99-100 and clean_shear_spec.m:74-75 use `isrow()`, which is true ONLY for a 1xN row vector and never for a genuine wide MxN matrix with M<N. So the port-faithfulness divergence is real: the Python heuristic would silently transpose any 2-D array with fewer rows than columns, whereas MATLAB would leave a wide matrix alone (and csd would then operate column-wise / error). Severity is correctly a nit, not higher, because it is unreachable in any valid pipeline run: (1) the production paths use csd_matrix_batch / clean_shear_spec_batch (l3.py, l3_chi.py, spectral.py:354-358), which require strict 3-D input and have NO auto-transpose heuristic; (2) the only non-batch callers are goodman.py internally and rsi/window.py:147, where shear/accel are already normalized samples-major (window.py:94-96) with diss_length (thousands of samples) >> n_shear (<=2 channels), so shape[0] is always >> shape[1]; (3) the `shape[0] < 2*nfft` length check on line 182 catches most residual misuse. The finding itself concedes impact is confined to external/edge use. Not addressed by PR #78 (which touches chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py — unrelated). The defect genuinely exists as a latent port-faithfulness/robustness divergence but corrupts no real output on any valid run, so nit is the honest severity.

confirmed.title: despike.py filtfilt uses scipy default padlen (6), not MATLAB convention (3) — latent port-divergence inconsistency with l2.py
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified the core factual claims against current code on this branch. despike.py:110 (`dv_hp = np.abs(filtfilt(b_hp, a_hp, dv_padded))`) and :114 (`dv_lp = filtfilt(b_lp, a_lp, dv_hp)`) both omit padlen, so scipy uses its default 3*max(len(a),len(b))=6 for these 1st-order Butterworth filters. odas/despike.m:184,192 call filtfilt(b,a,dv) with no padlen, which under MATLAB's convention is 3*(nfilt-1)=3. The sibling module scor160/l2.py:15-25 documents exactly this divergence in `_matlab_padlen()` and applies padlen=_matlab_padlen(...) to its own filtfilt calls (l2.py:198,277,283), so the inconsistency within the subpackage is real and despike.py was not given the same treatment. PR #78 does not touch despike.py (it changes only chi/chi.py, bottom.py, ct_align.py, profile.py), so the issue is unaffected by the fix branch.

However, the practical impact is essentially nil, justifying a downgrade from minor to nit: (1) Numeric impact — I reproduced a realistic 30 s section (15360 samples) and compared the dv_HP/dv_LP detection ratio with padlen=6 vs padlen=3; the max difference on the *used* (unpadded) range is ~3e-7. despike applies its own reflect-padding (pad_len = min(len, 2*int(fs/smooth)) ~2048) and discards it via the `rng` slice, so the filtfilt edge transient is fully absorbed and never reaches spike detection or the returned signal. The finding itself concedes this. (2) Crash — I confirmed length-1 and length-2 inputs raise ValueError with scipy padlen=6 (length-2 padded=6, not >6), whereas MATLAB padlen=3 survives length 2. But every caller (l2.py:123/145, l2_chi.py:105, viewer_base.py:580, diagnostics.py:132) feeds whole sections or whole signals that are constrained by minimum-duration section-selection criteria at fs~512 Hz; a 1-2 sample section is not producible on real data, so the crash is unreachable in practice. The defect is genuine (a correct, low-cost fix exists and the inconsistency is real) but it is a latent/cosmetic port-faithfulness nit with no effect on any valid run, not a minor functional bug.

confirmed.title: despike.py silently replaces data with 0.0 when a bad block spans the whole (padded) array — diverges from MATLAB (NaN) and hides data loss
confirmed.reasoning:
Confirmed against current branch code. despike.py lines 157-166 (_single_despike): when a contiguous bad region's before-region (line 148-151) and after-region (line 154-155) both yield no valid points, the if/elif chain falls through to `else: replacement = 0.0` (line 164), which is then written into the signal via `dv_padded[s_bad:e_bad] = replacement` (line 166).

Reachability empirically verified: with a constant baseline of 7.0 and a spike, arrays of length 5/6/7 (N=20 at fs=512) have the entire padded array marked bad (bad window s±N clamps to the whole array), both before/after regions empty, hitting line 164 and returning an all-0.0 signal (fraction=1.0, true baseline 7.0 destroyed). So the 0.0 branch genuinely executes and silently overwrites real data with a finite, plausible-looking value.

Port-faithfulness break confirmed: odas/despike.m lines 235-245 compute start_value/stop_value as `sum(points)/length(points)`; for an empty region this is `sum([])/length([]) = 0/0 = NaN`, and `(NaN+NaN)/2 = NaN`. MATLAB therefore yields NaN (visible as a gap to downstream QC), whereas Python yields 0.0 (masks the loss). Real divergence.

Not fixed by PR #78 (which touched chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — not despike.py); not a duplicate.

Severity: stays minor. The defect is real but cannot occur on the default/primary path: every despike call site that feeds primary results (scor160/l2.py, chi/l2_chi.py) operates on sections gated by profile_min_duration (default 10 s ≈ 5120 samples at 512 Hz), which dwarfs N (=20), so the whole-array-bad condition is unreachable through the pipeline. It only triggers via direct API use on a very short array or a misconfigured large N — off-nominal/edge, with silent corruption confined to those inputs. Not a blocker (no wrong primary result, no default crash) and not major (no real default output corrupted/lost), but more than a nit because it does cause silent data corruption when reached. The finding's own evidence already states this limited reachability honestly.

confirmed.title: despike.py spike-removal window uses N//2 (floor) where MATLAB uses round(N/2), giving a ~1-sample window difference for odd N
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED as a genuine port divergence, correctly rated nit. Read despike.py on this branch: line 93 `N_half = N // 2` (integer floor); lines 131-132 build the bad window as `good[max(0, s-N_half) : min(len, s+2*N_half+1)]`. Read odas/despike.m: line 166 `N = N/2;` (stays float for odd input N), line 217 `idx = round( max(1,s-N) : min(length(good_points),s+2*N) );` rounds the colon vector elementwise.

I reproduced both index sets numerically. For even user N (e.g. the DEFAULT path N=round(0.04*fs)=20 at fs=512) the two windows are identical (count match, same set). For odd user N they differ by exactly one sample on the HIGH edge only. Worked example (user N=15, Nf=7.5, spike s): MATLAB colon 92.5..115 rounds to s-7..s+15; Python yields s-7..s+14 — so MATLAB removes one extra sample after the spike. This holds for N=15, 21, 7 (all count diff = -1, high-edge-only).

Correction to the finding's evidence: its example claims the LOW edge also differs (MATLAB `s-8` vs Python `s-7`); that is wrong — both produce s-7 because round(92.5)=93=s-7. The divergence is high-edge-only (MATLAB s+15 vs Python s+14). The central claim (~1-sample window difference for odd N, default path even and exact) is correct despite this narrative imprecision.

Real-world reachability: caller l2.py line 121 sets `N_sh = round(sh_hw*fs) if sh_hw<1 else int(sh_hw)`; default despike_sh=[8.0,0.5,0.04] (io.py:451) gives round(0.04*fs), which is even at fs=512. Odd N only arises for non-default sh_hw/fs combinations supplied via the NetCDF despike_sh attribute. PR #78's four fixes (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py) do not touch despike.py, so the divergence is present on this branch. Physical impact is one sample at one spike boundary on off-default odd-N configs — negligible. Genuine defect, correctly a nit.

confirmed.title: gradT_noise / gradT_noise_batch / fp07_tau drop the MATLAB speed>0 validation, yielding negative noise, inf, or NaN for invalid speed
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL but downgraded minor->nit (purely latent; no live path triggers it).

Technical claims all reproduce on this branch:
- fp07_tau (fp07.py:120-130) does `0.01 * (1.0/speed)**0.5` for 'lueck' with no speed>0 guard. Confirmed: fp07_tau(-0.7,'lueck')=nan, fp07_tau(0.0,'lueck')=inf.
- gradT_noise (fp07.py:497) does `noise_K = noise_f / speed` with no guard. Confirmed: gradT_noise([10,20],10.0,-0.7) -> [-2.5e-08, -6.9e-08] (negative PSD, physically impossible); speed=0.0 -> [inf, inf].
- gradT_noise_batch (fp07.py:616) does `noise_f / speeds[:,np.newaxis]` likewise unguarded.

Port-faithfulness claim is correct: odas/gradT_noise_odas.m:157 has `val_speed = @(x) isnumeric(x) && all(x) > 0` registered via addRequired (line 164), which the Python port drops.

Export claim correct: fp07_tau and fp07_tau_batch are exported in chi/__init__.py (lines 18-19, 39-40).

CRITICAL severity context — the finding itself honestly states (and I confirmed) that EVERY live caller floors speed upstream, so no live pipeline path feeds invalid speed:
- rsi/window.py:100-103 sets W=mean(abs(speed)) then clamps to SPEED_MIN before fp07_tau (326) and gradT_noise (331,371).
- rsi/quick_look.py:72-74 sets mean_speed=mean(abs(...)) then floors to 0.01 before fp07_tau (149) and gradT_noise (150).
- chi/l3_chi.py:157-161 floors speed_means to 0.01 (with nan_to_num) before fp07_tau_batch (211) and gradT_noise_batch (223).
I grepped all of src/ for callers: these are the only three, and all three guard. So this is a pure defense-in-depth gap in public API functions, exercisable only by calling them directly with invalid speed outside the pipeline. PR #78 does not touch fp07.py, so it neither introduces nor fixes this.

Per the rubric ("nit = cosmetic/latent"), a defect with all live paths guarded and no off-nominal data the pipeline ever feeds it is latent, hence nit rather than minor. The defect genuinely exists (silent negative/inf/NaN from exported functions, diverging from the MATLAB reference's input validation), so real=true, but it does not affect any actual run.

confirmed.title: Broken-thermistor R_ratio clamp warns in scalar noise_thermchannel but is silent in noise_thermchannel_batch
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against the actual code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Scalar noise_thermchannel (fp07.py:435-441) emits warnings.warn("R_ratio=... < 0.1 (possible broken thermistor ...); clamped to 1.0") when R_ratio < 0.1, then sets R_ratio = 1.0. The vectorized noise_thermchannel_batch (fp07.py:574) performs the identical clamp via R_ratio = np.where(R_ratio < 0.1, 1.0, R_ratio) with NO warning. The production claim is accurate: l3_chi.py:223 calls gradT_noise_batch -> noise_thermchannel_batch (fp07.py:612), i.e. the silent path is used by the scor160/L3 chi pipeline; the scalar warning path is reached only via window.py:331/371 (rsi chi pipeline) and quick_look.py:150. Downstream formulas (R_actual=R_ratio*R_0, phi_R, scale_factor) are identical in both functions, so for any T_mean triggering the clamp both produce numerically identical output. Therefore this is purely a diagnostic-visibility inconsistency: a broken/abnormal thermistor that trips the clamp is surfaced in one pipeline but silently swallowed in the L3 batch pipeline. No numerical defect, no output corruption. Not touched by PR #78 (which modified chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py, not the fp07 noise functions). The defect genuinely holds but does not affect any computed result, so severity nit is correct.

confirmed.title: PFile._read() lacks short-header length guard, raising raw struct.error on truncated files
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. PFile._read() (lines 301-305) does `raw_hdr = f.read(HEADER_BYTES)` (HEADER_BYTES=128, line 27) then immediately calls _detect_endian(raw_hdr,...) and _parse_header(raw_hdr,...) with no length check. _detect_endian (line 70) does `struct.unpack_from(">H", raw_header, 63*2)` (offset 126), which raises struct.error on a buffer < 128 bytes. Reproduced: PFile on a 32-byte file raises `struct.error: unpack_from requires a buffer of at least 128 bytes...`. Confirmed struct.error.__mro__ = (struct.error, Exception, BaseException, object) — it is NOT a subclass of OSError/ValueError/RuntimeError. The batch handlers in convert.py (lines 605, 616) and cli.py (lines 354, 368, 410, 427) all catch only `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)`, so I directly demonstrated struct.error ESCAPES that tuple and would abort the batch loop. The sibling utility extract_pfile_segment (lines 128-130) DOES guard this exact case with `if len(raw_hdr) < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(...)`, confirming the defensive precedent exists but was not applied to the primary reader. PR #78's fix commit 1a78fca touched chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py — NOT p_file.py — so this is unfixed and independent. The existing test tests/test_p_file_corrupt.py::test_tiny_file_raises (lines 72-77) asserts `pytest.raises((ValueError, struct.error))`, accepting BOTH error types — it documents, rather than refutes, the leaky behavior; passing does not protect callers that only catch the cleaner exceptions. DOWNGRADE major->minor: this never affects a valid run's primary scientific result and is not silent corruption. It is off-nominal/edge: it requires a malformed/truncated input under 128 bytes (itself an error condition), and the failure is LOUD (a struct.error traceback), not silent. The real consequence is that one sub-header-sized corrupt file aborts a batch loop instead of being logged-and-skipped like every other bad-file case. That is a genuine robustness gap worth the one-line guard, but it is edge-case batch-resilience, not a blocker or silent data loss, so minor is the honest calibration.

confirmed.title: convert_all batch loops fail to catch struct.error from corrupt/truncated .p files, aborting the whole batch
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL on this branch and not touched by PR #78 (commit 1a78fca only edits chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py). Mechanism confirmed by reading and reproducing:

1) struct.error MRO is [error, Exception, BaseException, object] — it is NOT a subclass of OSError/ValueError/RuntimeError. Both convert_all loops catch only `except (OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` (convert.py:605 serial, :616 parallel via future.result()).

2) PFile._read() (p_file.py:301-305) reads HEADER_BYTES then immediately calls _detect_endian()/_parse_header() with NO length guard — unlike the sibling extract_pfile_segment() which explicitly guards `if len(raw_hdr) < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError` (p_file.py:128-130). On a sub-128-byte file, _detect_endian()'s struct.unpack_from at byte offset 126 (p_file.py:70) raises struct.error.

3) Reproduced directly: a 10-byte file -> PFile() raises `struct.error: unpack_from requires a buffer of at least 128 bytes ... (actual buffer size is 10)`. `convert_all([tiny.p], out, jobs=1)` and `jobs=2` BOTH abort with that struct.error instead of log-and-continue.

4) The log-and-continue contract is deliberate and tested: tests/test_hard_batch_branches.py:278 (test_serial_logs_failure_on_error) asserts the serial loop logs and continues — but only exercises a ValueError, so the struct.error gap is untested. tests/test_p_file_corrupt.py:72-77 (test_tiny_file_raises) already uses `pytest.raises((ValueError, struct.error))`, confirming the project knows PFile can raise struct.error on truncated input. Truncated files are a documented real condition (p_file.py:354: interrupted DAQ / truncated download).

The finding's primary claim (convert.py:605/616) is fully correct. Its secondary CLI claim is imprecise: cli.py:253's `except (FileNotFoundError, OSError, ValueError)` belongs to _cmd_cutp, NOT the nc subcommand; _cmd_nc (cli.py:212-223) has no try/except at all, so the struct.error propagates out of `rsi-tpw nc` as a traceback regardless — the stated end-user impact holds, only the cited line/wrapper is wrong.

Severity recalibrated major->minor: it is edge-triggered (requires a corrupt/truncated .p file) and fails LOUDLY with a traceback (not silent corruption of good output), so it does not meet the major bar of silent loss/corruption. It is a genuine, reproducible robustness gap that defeats a deliberately-designed-and-tested per-file log-and-continue contract — one bad file kills the whole batch and suppresses output for all other valid files in the same invocation — which keeps it above nit.

confirmed.title: Corrupt record_size/n_cols produces cryptic 'cannot reshape array' crash instead of a clear diagnostic
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against the actual code on this branch. In `_read()` (p_file.py:361-379) the loader computes `data_words = (record_size - header_size) // 2` (line 361), `scans_per_record = data_words // self.n_cols` (377), `total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record` (378), then `records["data"].reshape(total_scans, self.n_cols)` (379). `records["data"]` holds `n_records * data_words` elements but the target shape requests `n_records * (data_words // n_cols) * n_cols` elements; when `data_words % n_cols != 0` these differ and reshape raises `ValueError: cannot reshape array`. I reproduced the exact cited case: record_size=256, header_size=128 -> data_words=64, fast_cols=3 -> 64 % 3 = 1, yielding `ValueError: cannot reshape array of size 320 into shape (105,3)`. I also confirmed the asymmetry the finding cites: `extract_pfile_segment` (p_file.py:138-148) validates header_size >= HEADER_BYTES, config_size >= 0, record_size > 0, and record_size >= header_size, whereas `_read()` (lines 301-379) performs NO geometry validation before the reshape — its only guards are `n_records < 1` (350) and a partial-record warning (352-359), neither of which catches `data_words % n_cols != 0`. PR #78 does not touch this path (its fixes are in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py), so it neither causes nor resolves this. The defect is genuine, so real=true. However I downgrade minor->nit: this only triggers on a corrupt/truncated/non-RSI header (valid RSI .p files always have (record_size-header_size)/2 an integer multiple of n_cols by DAQ construction), so it never affects a valid run's scientific output, causes no silent corruption and no data loss — the file fails to load either way; the sole effect is numpy's wording of the ValueError instead of a domain-specific 'corrupt record_size' message. That is a diagnostic-quality / latent-robustness improvement, i.e. a nit, not a minor functional bug.

confirmed.title: Zero n_cols or n_rows in a corrupt header causes bare ZeroDivisionError
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL on this branch. In PFile._read() (p_file.py), line 317 sets self.n_cols = self.fast_cols + self.slow_cols and line 318 sets self.n_rows = self.header["n_rows"], then line 322 does `self.fs_fast = f_clock / self.n_cols` and line 323 does `self.fs_slow = self.fs_fast / self.n_rows`, with no validation in between. I reproduced both branches with synthetic headers: fast_cols=slow_cols=0 raises `ZeroDivisionError: division by zero` at line 322 (no file context); n_rows=0 raises the same at line 323. By contrast, the separate extract_pfile_segment function (lines 119-176) does validate its header_size/config_size/record_size fields and raises descriptive ValueErrors — so the unguarded divisors here are an inconsistency, exactly as the evidence states.

Additional fact strengthening the finding: the batch driver convert_all (convert.py lines 600-617) wraps each file in `except (OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` and logs `f"{p_path.name}: {e}"` to continue past bad files. I confirmed ZeroDivisionError is a subclass of ArithmeticError, NOT of OSError/ValueError/RuntimeError, so it escapes this handler — one corrupt file aborts the entire batch and the file-naming log line never executes. This is the worst-case manifestation of the finding's "no file context, hard batch triage" concern.

Severity stays minor (not major): this triggers only on a CORRUPT/malformed header — real VMP/RSI .p files always carry non-zero matrix geometry (8x10 for SN479) — so it never affects a valid run, and the result is a crash on bad input, not silent corruption or loss of real output. It is more than a nit because it defeats the intended log-and-continue batch robustness. Not fixed by PR #78 (which touches chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py, not p_file.py); git history shows no validation guard was added in _read. Original severity "minor" is correctly calibrated.

confirmed.title: GRADT temperature-gradient computation crashes on a single-sample fast channel
confirmed.reasoning:
Verified the cited code at convert.py:508-517 matches the finding exactly: inside the GRADT pre-compute loop, `dTdt = np.diff(T_fast_ch) * pf.fs_fast` followed by `dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1])`. I reproduced the mechanism in isolation: with a 1-element input, `np.diff` returns a size-0 array and `dTdt[-1]` raises `IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0`. So the unguarded indexing defect genuinely exists and is a latent crash.

However, reachability on real data is effectively nil, which is why nit is the correct severity. A fast channel's length is `matrix_count * self.n_rows` (p_file.py:440, 475). The real ARCTERX VMP-250 instrument has an 8-row address matrix (CLAUDE.md; n_rows from header word at p_file.py:318), so the minimum fast-channel length is 8 even when only one scan is present (matrix_count==1) — `np.diff` on 8 samples gives 7 and `dTdt[-1]` is fine. Triggering size-1 requires BOTH matrix_count==1 AND n_rows==1, i.e. a hand-crafted degenerate 1-row-matrix, single-scan .p file that also declares a temperature-gradient channel. PFile only enforces n_records>=1 (p_file.py:350), which is insufficient to prevent this synthetic case but no genuine instrument data produces it. The finding itself honestly self-classifies as a nit and states it is reachable only for a pathological ~1-scan file.

PR #78 does not touch convert.py (`git diff main...fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors -- src/odas_tpw/rsi/convert.py` is empty; the block was last modified in an unrelated rename commit b11117a), so the finding is neither fixed nor mooted by the PR and stands on its own. Conclusion: real defect, correctly severity-rated as nit — a latent robustness gap on a degenerate input that real runs never hit, not an off-nominal path reachable in practice.

confirmed.title: Joined 32-bit (2-id) channels never get ODAS's signed-by-default correction
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED ACCURATE but downgraded to nit (latent, no current effect). Read the real code on this branch. p_file.py:471-473 joins 2-id channels as unsigned `odd_data*2**16 + even_data` (both words wrapped to positive via `+= 2**16`). The sign block p_file.py:503-523 only ever performs the 16-bit unsigned wrap (int16 view / `raw[raw<0] += 2**16`); grep confirms no `2**31`/`2**32` signed-32-bit correction anywhere in p_file.py (the only 2**31/2**32 hits are timezone). convert_raw in channels.py is a pure passthrough, so no downstream correction either. ODAS read_odas.m:360-366 joins identically, then read_odas.m:371-398 runs a sign-correction loop over ALL channels: jac_c/sbt/sbc/o2_43f are `continue`d (skipped, line 381-385), jac_t forced unsigned, sign=unsigned does +2^16, and the DEFAULT else branch applies `ch(ch>=2^31) -= 2^32` (lines 392-396), i.e. every joined 32-bit channel is signed-by-default. So the port genuinely diverges from ODAS for a signed raw 32-bit channel with the high bit set — the defect is real. HOWEVER the practical impact is nil: the only 2-id channel in the real ARCTERX/SN479 data is JAC_C (type=jac_c, confirmed via strings on VMP/*.p), which ODAS explicitly skips, keeping it unsigned-joined — exactly matching Python. So there is zero numerical divergence on every valid run of the actual dataset. The unit test test_p_file_branches.py:355 (test_two_id_channel_decoded) uses type=raw (not in the skip set) but only asserts the channel exists, not its signed values, so it would not catch the divergence — confirming the latent-only nature. Not touched by PR #78 (which only edits chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py). Real defect, but it produces no wrong result on any current valid run and only affects a hypothetical future signed-32-bit config, which is below minor (off-nominal/edge) — it is a latent port-faithfulness gap, i.e. nit. A documenting comment per the suggested fix would suffice.

confirmed.title: Bottom-crash sample-mean can under-trim by up to half a bin width when the crash onset is early in the flagged bin
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL at minor. bottom.py:135-136 returns `float(np.nanmean(depth[in_range & (idx == i)]))` — the mean depth of all samples that fell in the flagged (deepest spiking) bin. Because the profiler keeps descending through the crash, the flagged bin accumulates samples both before and after onset, so the mean lands near the bin center. When the crash onset is in the shallow half of the bin, the reported mean lies BELOW (deeper than) the onset. The caller (perturb/pipeline.py:432-438) trims at `P_seg >= bottom_depth` then `candidate = new_s + below[0] - 1`, so it keeps everything shallower than the reported depth, leaving the onset-to-mean tail (up to ~half a bin ≈ 2 m at the default depth_window=4) of contaminated samples un-trimmed. The arithmetic in the evidence checks out (deepest bin [46,50), profile to 49.5 m, onset 46.2 m -> mean ≈ 47.75 m -> ~1.55 m kept). Severity stays MINOR, not higher: (1) the under-trim is bounded to ~half a bin near the very deepest point; (2) bottom trimming is OPT-IN — pipeline.py:353 `do_bottom = bottom_cfg.get("enable", False)` defaults off — so it never touches the default epsilon/chi result; (3) the mean was a deliberate, defensible fix for the prior catastrophic 'geometric-center overhangs nanmax -> matches nothing -> no trim at all' bug (lines 126-134 comment), trading a half-bin granularity limit for guaranteed trimmability. The suggested fix (report the bin minimum or sub-bin to find the rise edge) is a reasonable refinement but the current behavior is a documented fundamental resolution limit, not silent corruption of real output. test_processing_bottom.py::test_reports_sample_mean_within_flagged_bin only exercises a centered interior-bin spike, so the early-onset under-trim is genuinely untested, supporting the finding's premise without contradicting the minor severity.

confirmed.title: chi variance-correction grid is fixed at 80000 points per call regardless of n_fine, ~40x the prior size
confirmed.reasoning: Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors (PR #78). chi.py:113 is `n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB)` with _N_FINE=2000, _K_SPAN_KB=40, _PTS_PER_KB=2000, so n = max(2000, 80000) = 80000 unconditionally (n_fine floor is never binding). Confirmed the prior grid: HEAD~1 (commit before PR #78) used `K_fine = np.linspace(..., n_fine)` with n_fine=2000, so PR #78 grew the grid exactly 2000 -> 80000 = 40x, matching the finding. Each call builds two 80000-element float64 arrays plus a grad_func + H2 evaluation; I measured ~0.93 ms/call (finding claims ~0.96 ms — matches). It is called up to 3x per window in the iterative fit loop (chi.py:588 inside `for iteration in range(3)`), plus chi.py:261 (Method 1) and 440. The over-resolution claim is also correct: I compared correction values across points-per-kB against a 20000-pts/kB reference for kB in {2,5,10,20,30}; even at 200 pts/kB the worst-case error (kB=30) is 0.011%, far under 0.1%, so _PTS_PER_KB=2000 over-resolves by ~10x and a few hundred pts/kB would suffice. This is purely a performance/resolution inefficiency: chi correctness is unaffected (the variance-correction ratio is converged to <0.0001% at this resolution), there is no silent corruption, no crash, no output change. The PR #78 fix that introduced this (correctly fixing a kB<<K_max bias by spanning 40*kB) chose an over-generous point density. Severity nit is accurate — real but low-priority cleanup (lower _PTS_PER_KB or cap n).

confirmed.title: compute_trim_depth returns the shallowest sub-threshold bin instead of the prop-wash exit, so a momentarily-quiet top bin causes silent under-trim that leaves the noisy near-surface section in the profile
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed real by reading the code and reproducing it empirically on this branch (top_trim.py untouched by PR #78, whose fixes are in chi/chi.py, profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py). The per-channel loop at lines 97-100 (`for i in range(len(std_arr)): if isfinite(std_arr[i]) and std_arr[i] < threshold: trim_depths.append(bin_centers[i]); break`) returns the FIRST (shallowest) bin below threshold and breaks. Line 106 then takes np.max across channels, but each channel already committed to its shallowest crossing, so the max cannot recover a deeper exit. This directly contradicts the module's own docstring (top_trim.py lines 6-7: "reports the deepest depth at which all channels have settled below their own quantile threshold") and the __init__.py description (line 9-10: "prop-wash exit"). I reproduced the finding's exact scenario: depth 0-50 m, quiet baseline, strong noise injected only over 5-15 m, quiet top bin — compute_trim_depth(..., dz=1.0, min_depth=1.0, max_depth=50.0, quantile=0.6) returns 1.0 m, leaving the entire 5-15 m noisy section in the profile. A minimal "quiet bin [1,2), then noise ramps 2-12 m" variant also collapses to 1.0 m. By contrast the canonical monotonic-decay-from-top case (matching the existing passing test test_high_variance_at_top) correctly returns ~13 m, which is why the bug is latent in tests. The caller (perturb/pipeline.py:394-403) faithfully translates trim_depth into a start-index push with `above = np.where(P_seg >= trim_depth)[0]`, so the under-trim propagates into the production perturb cast; there is no compensating re-trim in the caller and only a downstream per-segment FOM cut (pipeline.py:454+) that partially and indirectly mitigates spectral-fit contamination, not a substitute for the trim. The finding's secondary point that the threshold (line 95) is the 0.6 quantile of the channel's own std including the noisy top bins, inflating it and making an early quiet bin more likely to qualify, is also accurate but a softer contributor. Severity kept at major (not blocker): the failure is data-dependent — the common free-fall case where vibration is highest at the very first bin and decays monotonically works correctly — but when a quiet bin sits above the noisy region (plausible at release before descent rate / prop-wash stabilizes) the result is a silent under-trim that retains the noisiest near-surface data and contaminates epsilon/chi in those bins with no error, meeting the silent-corruption-of-real-output bar for major. Suggested fix (find deepest exceedance and trim just below it, plus a quiet-top/noisy-middle regression test) is sound.

confirmed.title: detect_bottom_crash emits a spurious 'All-NaN slice encountered' RuntimeWarning on its intended-silent all-NaN-depth bail-out path (np.errstate does not suppress nanmax's warnings.warn-issued warning)
confirmed.reasoning: Core technical claim is TRUE and verified at runtime. bottom.py:89-90 wraps `max_depth = np.nanmax(depth)` in `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore"):` with a comment (lines 91-92) stating the intent is to bail out cleanly on an all-NaN depth segment. But `np.errstate(invalid="ignore")` only masks IEEE FP error flags; numpy's "All-NaN slice encountered" message is issued via `warnings.warn(RuntimeWarning)`, which errstate does NOT intercept. I confirmed by importing the function: `detect_bottom_crash(np.full(1000,np.nan), {"Ax": rand}, fs=512.0)` raises RuntimeWarning under `-W error::RuntimeWarning` and emits the warning under warnings.catch_warnings, while still correctly returning None. The warning fires ONLY on the fully-all-NaN path; normal and partial-NaN depth inputs emit no warning (verified). So the suggested fix (short-circuit `if not np.any(np.isfinite(depth)): return None`, or use `warnings.catch_warnings`) is valid. HOWEVER, the finding is overstated on three counts, justifying a downgrade from minor to nit: (1) The cited test `test_all_nan_depth_returns_none` does NOT exist; the real bottom-crash tests (tests/test_easy_batch_branches.py::TestBottomCrashShortBins and tests/test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py::TestM4BottomCrashDepth) never pass all-NaN depth and pass cleanly even under `-W error::RuntimeWarning`, so the claim that an existing test already trips this is false. (2) pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] filterwarnings has only two `ignore` deprecation entries and NO `error` filter, so warnings are not promoted to errors in this project's suite. (3) The sole production caller (src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:414-431) wraps the call in `try/except Exception` and feeds `depth_seg = P_fast[s_fast:e_fast]`; since profiles are detected from pressure, an all-NaN pressure segment yielding a valid profile range is improbable, and even a caller that promoted warnings could not crash the pipeline. Impact is therefore cosmetic log noise on a rare/edge path that already returns the correct value — a latent/cosmetic nit, not a minor functional defect. Not fixed by PR #78 (the 4 fixes touch a different concern in bottom.py — the deepest-bin mean-depth reporting at lines 124-136 — not this suppression mechanism).

confirmed.title: mypy per-module overrides reference three nonexistent modules (config drift), silently suppressing nothing
confirmed.reasoning: Verified on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. pyproject.toml lines 64-80 list `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` module entries; lines 65-67 are "odas_tpw.rsi.spectral", "odas_tpw.rsi.ocean", "odas_tpw.rsi.nasmyth". `find src/odas_tpw/rsi -name spectral*/ocean*/nasmyth*` returns nothing — these three files do not exist under rsi/. The real consolidated modules exist under scor160/ (src/odas_tpw/scor160/spectral.py, ocean.py, nasmyth.py) and are correctly listed at lines 77-79. The other rsi entries in the override list (deconvolve, profile, helpers, convert at lines 68-71) all exist as real files, confirming these three are uniquely stale. `grep -rn "rsi.spectral|rsi.ocean|rsi.nasmyth|from .spectral|..."` over src/odas_tpw/rsi/ returns no hits, so nothing imports them. mypy silently ignores overrides for modules it never analyzes, so the disable_error_code block (line 83) is a no-op for these three names. The defect is REAL and accurately described, but the severity is OVERSTATED. The original evidence even concedes it is "benign at runtime." There is zero impact on epsilon/chi computation, no crash, no silent output corruption, no masked real suppression (the named modules don't exist so nothing is being under-type-checked by that name). It is pure cosmetic config drift / latent cleanliness — squarely a nit, not minor. Not touched by PR #78 (which targets chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py).

confirmed.title: parse_config crashes with a bare ValueError on a non-integer matrix entry or channel id in a corrupt/truncated config string
confirmed.reasoning: Verified the cited code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Line 249: `result["matrix"].append([int(x) for x in val.split()])`. Line 390: `ids = [int(x) for x in ch["id"].replace(",", " ").split()]`. Both are exactly as described, with no try/except anywhere in p_file.py (grep confirms only `parse_config` call at 313 and no `except`). Line 312 decodes the config with `errors="replace"`, so a corrupt/truncated record-0 can genuinely yield non-numeric tokens. I reproduced both crashes empirically: a matrix row `1 2 x 4` raises `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'x'` and a channel id `1, q, 3` raises the analogous bare ValueError — neither includes the filename. This is a real, distinct defect from the already-confirmed binary-header (n_cols/header_size) crashes since it lives in the ASCII config parse path. PR #78 does not touch p_file.py (git diff main...HEAD lists chi.py, bottom.py, ct_align.py, profile.py only), so the issue stands on this branch. However the severity is overstated. It cannot trigger on any valid .p file (clean configs have integer matrix rows and channel ids), so it never affects a normal run; on corrupt input the code already fails loudly with a ValueError (no silent corruption, no wrong result), and the only actual deficiency is a missing filename in the exception message — inconsistent with the surrounding convention at lines 350-359 which prefix errors/warnings with self.filepath.name. That is a latent diagnostic-quality issue with zero behavioral or data consequence, which is a nit, not a minor.

confirmed.title: Deconvolve sign-inversion branch (p[0] < -0.5, which negates the entire pre-emphasized signal) has zero test coverage
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL but downgraded minor->nit. The branch exists exactly as cited: deconvolve.py:66-68 `if p[0] < -0.5: X_dX = -X_dX.astype(np.float64)`, a faithful port of odas/deconvolve.m:189 (`if p(1)<-0.5, X_dX=-X_dX; end`); p[0]/p(1) both correctly denote the polyfit slope (highest-order coeff first in both numpy and MATLAB). The test gap is confirmed: tests/test_deconvolve.py (8 tests, all passing) exercises only positively-correlated X_dX = lfilter(b,a,X_true), so polyfit slope p[0] > 0 and the < -0.5 branch never runs; grep for -0.5/invert/negat/p[0]/astype/32768 in the test file returns no relevant matches. The promotion guard is genuinely load-bearing on the real data path: I traced the caller p_file.py:588 -> X_dX_raw = channels_raw[dX_name]; for single-id fast channels (the FP07 T1_dT1 case) channels_raw holds the raw int16 slice (raw_flat from records['data'], dtype '>i2'/'<i2', p_file.py:362,379,448), so without .astype(np.float64) a -32768 sample would wrap to -32768 under negation and silently sign-corrupt the deconvolved signal. This is NOT a current bug (the code is correct), NOT fixed by PR #78 (which touched chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py - not deconvolve.py), and NOT a duplicate. However, per the verifier rubric it is a pure test gap for a correct, rarely-triggered branch that affects no actual run today (nit = cosmetic/latent), not an off-nominal defect in shipping output (minor). Real, but nit-severity.

confirmed.title: csd_matrix accepts a length-1/scalar custom window and silently broadcasts it to a rectangular window (no nfft-length validation)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified true on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. spectral.py:201 does `window = _get_window(nfft) if window is None else np.asarray(window, dtype=np.float64)` with NO length validation, and the window is then applied via `_detrend_segment(...) * window` (lines 214, 235, 237). The MATLAB reference odas/csd_matrix_odas.m:124-126 explicitly errors: `if ~isvector(Window) || (size(Window,1) ~= n_fft) error('Window must be empty or a vector of length n_fft')`. So the port omits a check present in the reference. I empirically reproduced the silent failure: csd_matrix(x,None,256,100.0,window=np.array([2.0])) returns shape (129,1,1) with no error (a length-1 window broadcasts to a uniform scale factor, i.e. a rectangular/un-windowed spectrum scaled by 2). A 0-d scalar window also broadcasts silently. A wrong-length non-scalar window (e.g. length 100) already raises a numpy broadcast ValueError, so only scalar/length-1 windows are silently wrong. Impact is correctly bounded to external API misuse: I checked every internal caller (csd_matrix in goodman.py:150,168 with no window arg; csd_odas in rsi/window.py:183,365 with no window arg; csd_matrix_batch in chi/l3_chi.py and scor160/l3.py has no window param at all) — all use window=None, so no default-path or any current internal computation is affected. The `window=` keyword is public/documented ("Window of length nfft") so the hazard is genuine but only triggers if an external caller passes a malformed scalar window. Not addressed by PR #78 (fixes were in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — not spectral.py). Genuine latent robustness gap with no impact on real output; original `minor` severity is accurate.

confirmed.title: csd_matrix_batch omits the 2*nfft minimum-length requirement enforced by csd_matrix and the MATLAB reference (allows statistically-weak single-segment spectra)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified all factual claims against current code on fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. (1) Non-batch csd_matrix DOES enforce the 2*nfft minimum: spectral.py:182-183 `if x.shape[0] < 2 * nfft: raise ValueError("Input length ... must be at least 2*nfft ...")`. (2) The MATLAB reference matches: odas/csd_matrix_odas.m:110 `if (size(x,1) < 2*n_fft), ...` (error). (3) The batch path only guards `n_seg < 1` at spectral.py:372-375, with n_seg computed at line 371 as `(diss_length - overlap)//step`. I confirmed numerically that for default overlap=nfft//2 (step=nfft//2) a diss_length in [nfft, 2*nfft) yields n_seg in {1,2} and passes the batch check while failing the non-batch/MATLAB 2*nfft check (e.g. diss_length=256, nfft=256 -> n_seg=1 passes batch but fails 2*nfft). So the batch and non-batch paths genuinely diverge, and clean_shear_spec_batch (the batch caller in goodman.py:238) inherits the looser check. (4) The gap is reachable in principle: io.py:551 reads `diss_length = int(attrs["diss_length"])` directly from NetCDF attributes with no >=2*fft_length validation, so an externally-produced NetCDF could drive a single-segment spectrum that the non-batch/MATLAB path would reject. (5) Test gap confirmed: tests/test_scor160_spectral.py has test_too_short_raises for csd_matrix (line 193-196, match "at least 2*nfft") but TestCsdMatrixBatch has no equivalent. (6) PR #78 does not touch spectral.py (git diff main...HEAD shows only chi/chi.py, processing/bottom.py, processing/ct_align.py, rsi/profile.py). Severity correctly stays MINOR: on the standard l3 processing path diss_length is many multiples of nfft (io.py fallbacks use 2*fft_length / 4*fft_length, ODAS convention >> nfft), so it is not triggered by a normal valid run; even when triggered it does not crash and does not corrupt a correct result — it produces a low-DOF single-segment spectrum on off-nominal/external input. This is a genuine port-faithfulness/robustness gap (off-nominal/edge), not a blocker or silent-corruption-on-valid-run major.

confirmed.title: write_patched_pfile lacks the header_size >= HEADER_BYTES guard, raising a cryptic struct.error on a corrupt/small header_size
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL via reproduction. In write_patched_pfile (config_patch.py:504-528), header_size = words[_H["header_size"]] is read but never validated against HEADER_BYTES. _H["config_size"]=11 (p_file.py:41), so line 528 does struct.pack_into(f"{endian}H", header_region, 22, ...) into header_region = bytearray(f.read(header_size)) (line 527). When header_size is in [12,23], the buffer is shorter than the required 24 bytes (offset 22 + 2). The new PR-area guard at line 521 (first_record_size >= file_size) does NOT prevent this: with header_size=20 and a small config in a >24-byte file, first_record_size < file_size passes, and execution reaches line 527-528. I crafted a little-endian .p with header_version major=6, config_size=4, header_size=20, plus 4 KiB of data (first_record_size=24 < file_size=4228) and called write_patched_pfile — it raised exactly: struct.error: pack_into requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes for packing 2 bytes at offset 22 (actual buffer size is 20). _cmd_patch_config (cli.py:291) only catches (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError); struct.error is not a subclass of any of these, so it escapes as a full uncaught traceback, bypassing the deliberate clean "Error: {e}" path. This is inconsistent with extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138) which raises a clean ValueError on header_size < HEADER_BYTES. Caveats keeping it at minor (not higher): (1) trigger is strictly off-nominal/corrupt input — real RSI .p files have header_size=128; (2) no silent corruption or wrong result on valid data — the data-corruption guard at line 521 is present; the symptom is only a cryptic crash vs a clean message. The finding's secondary claim that read_config_text "shares the same unvalidated read" is slightly overstated: it does f.seek(header_size)/f.read(config_size) (line 484-485) which reads wrong bytes but does NOT raise struct.error. PR #78's four fixes are in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — config_patch.py is untouched, so the defect is live on this branch. Original severity "minor" is accurate: a genuine off-nominal robustness/error-propagation gap that defeats the CLI's intended error handling, at the boundary with nit.

confirmed.title: write_patched_pfile is not atomic: a mid-copy failure leaves a partial patched .p in the final destination with no cleanup
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL, severity correctly calibrated as minor. I read config_patch.py:494-542 (write_patched_pfile): it opens the FINAL destination directly with `open(dst, "xb")` (line 534) and streams source data in 1 MiB chunks (lines 537-541) with NO try/except, NO temp-file-then-rename, and NO unlink-on-failure. The body precisely matches the finding's description. I confirmed the caller chain: patch_files (config_patch.py:657) calls write_patched_pfile with no wrapper, and _cmd_patch_config (cli.py:291-293) catches OSError but only prints "Error" and sys.exit(1) — it never removes the partial dst. So a mid-stream write failure (disk full, I/O error, SIGINT) after the header+config are written leaves a truncated .p in place. NOT fixed by PR #78: this branch's 4 fixes touch chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py — not config_patch.py; the `xb` exclusive-create (0dc89c7, cited in the finding) closes the TOCTOU window but does not make the write atomic against mid-stream failure. The finding's claim is accurate. Two facts keep this at minor rather than higher: (1) the reader mitigates the "valid-looking" concern — PFile derives n_records from actual file size (p_file.py:349) rather than the header, and warns when file_size is not an integer multiple of record_size (p_file.py:352-359), so the common non-boundary-aligned 1 MiB-chunk truncation IS flagged; only a truncation that happens to land exactly on a record boundary would be silent. (2) This is an opt-in maintenance utility (patch-config), not part of the default epsilon/chi pipeline, it triggers only on an off-nominal I/O failure, and the original source file is never modified. Per the rubric this is off-nominal/edge = minor, matching the original severity. It is not major (no silent corruption of real output on a valid run) and not a blocker.

confirmed.title: patch_files aborts the whole batch (and leaves the first output written) when two source files share a basename
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against the current branch. In patch_files (config_patch.py:631-657) the loop builds `dst = out_dir / src.name` (line 633), using only the basename, with no up-front collision detection. It writes each file in turn; before the write it does `if dst.exists(): raise FileExistsError(f"{dst} already exists; remove it or choose another --out")` (lines 655-656). So when two inputs share a basename, the first is written to out_dir/NAME.p, the second raises FileExistsError mid-loop, and _cmd_patch_config (cli.py:291-293) catches it and exits(1). The remaining inputs are never processed, the first output is left on disk, and the message blames a stale output rather than the real basename collision — all four claims in the finding hold. The collision scenario is realistic: _resolve_p_files (cli.py:31-47) accepts arbitrary, multi-directory globs, and RSI .p files use per-deployment serial numbering (e.g. ARCTERX_..._SN479_0001.p), so two deployment directories readily contain same-basename files; combining them in one patch-config invocation into a shared --out triggers it. However this is the manual, occasional patch-config utility, not the default epsilon/chi processing path. The failure is loud (non-zero exit + stderr), not silent; the first file is correctly patched (no corruption); and the half-finished state is fully recoverable. It requires an uncommon cross-directory usage pattern. Not touched by PR #78 (config_patch.py came from PR #77, commit 5465242; the PR #78 fix commit 1a78fca does not modify it), so not fixed and not a duplicate of a PR #78 fix. No test covers the in-batch basename collision (tests in TestPatchFiles use distinct basenames; test_existing_dst_refused only covers re-running into a populated dir). A genuine but edge-case robustness/usability defect; original 'minor' severity is well-calibrated.

confirmed.title: round(v, 10) collapses tiny float config values (epsilon_minimum/chi_minimum = 1e-13) to 0.0, corrupting the version hash and signature provenance
confirmed.reasoning: The technical claims are all verified on this branch and PR #78 does not touch config_base.py (git log main..HEAD shows it unchanged). _normalize_value line 38 is `return round(v, 10)`; round(1e-13,10)==0.0 and round(5e-13,10)==0.0, and the v==int(v) branch (line 36) does not catch 1e-13. perturb DEFAULTS set epsilon_minimum=1e-13 (config.py:94) and chi_minimum=1.0e-13 (config.py:125). epsilon/chi are NOT in _HASH_EXCLUDE_KEYS (only 'diagnostics') nor _DYNAMIC_KEY_SECTIONS (only 'instruments'), so these floors flow through _canonicalize_section -> _normalize_nested -> _normalize_value. End-to-end verified: compute_hash('epsilon',{'epsilon_minimum':1e-13})==compute_hash('epsilon',{'epsilon_minimum':5e-13}) (both canonicalize epsilon_minimum to 0.0), and pipeline.py:1648/1661 feed eps_params/chi_params (incl. the floor) into resolve_output_dir for the diss/chi dirs, so distinct floors in [-5e-11,5e-11] collide to one output dir and reuse stale binned.nc. Provenance disagreement also confirmed concretely: write_resolved_config writes `epsilon_minimum: 1e-13` to config.yaml while the .params_sha256 signature JSON records 0.0. test_config.py:68 (test_fractional_float_rounded) does assert round(v,10) for a 0.12-magnitude value, and no test covers the tiny-value collapse. The suggested fix float(f'{v:.10e}') verified to keep 1e-13/5e-13 distinct, collapse 256.0->256 via the earlier branch, and round-trip 0.123456789012345 -> 0.12345678901. DOWNGRADE major->minor: (1) it does NOT corrupt the primary scientific result — the floor actually applied in mk_epsilon_mean uses eps_cfg.get('epsilon_minimum') = the true 1e-13 (pipeline.py:1264), not 0.0; only the cache key and the signature record are wrong. (2) A default run is internally self-consistent (1e-13 always hashes the same dir). (3) The stale-reuse harm requires an uncommon trigger: changing the floor from one sub-5e-11 value to a *different* sub-5e-11 value. This is an off-nominal cache-keying/provenance defect, not silent loss of real output on a valid run, so minor rather than major.

confirmed.title: Sequential output-dir glob `{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]` only matches 2-digit suffixes; once >100 distinct configs accumulate, dir 100 is invisible to the scan, causing reuse-failure and a hard data-corruption collision
confirmed.reasoning:
VERIFIED PRESENT on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. PR #78 did not touch config_base.py (no entries in `git log main..HEAD -- src/odas_tpw/config_base.py`), so the defect is fully live. Read config_base.py:223-259: resolve_output_dir scans with `pattern = str(base / f"{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]")` (line 241, exactly two `[0-9]` classes) but creates dirs with `{next_seq:02d}` (line 256), which emits 3 digits for next_seq>=100. The two-digit glob therefore cannot match `eps_100`.

Reproduced end-to-end with the real `odas_tpw.rsi.config.resolve_output_dir`: after creating 101 distinct configs (eps_00..eps_100), a 102nd distinct config (fft_length=6000) resolves to eps_100 because the scan misses eps_100, max_seq recomputes to 99, next_seq=100, and `mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` (line 257) silently reuses the existing dir. eps_100 then holds TWO conflicting `.params_sha256_*` signature files (hashes 3d65f0512361 for fft=1100 and 833122b8aa05 for fft=6000) and two parameter sets' NetCDF outputs co-mingled. This is genuine silent violation of the one-dir-per-config-signature invariant.

HOWEVER one sub-claim is FACTUALLY WRONG: the finding states a re-run of the eps_100 config "fails to find it and creates a fresh eps_01, orphaning eps_100's data." My repro shows the re-run of fft=1100 resolves BACK to eps_100 (next_seq recomputes to 100 -> mkdir(exist_ok=True) reuse path, and the original matching signature file is still present), NOT a fresh eps_01. The data is not orphaned to eps_01; it stays in eps_100. The real harm is collision/co-mingling of two distinct configs, not orphaning.

Severity recalibrated major->minor: (1) the trigger requires >100 DISTINCT parameter signatures in a single output_root — each resolve_output_dir call is per-section and a new dir is created only per distinct signature, so this is reached only by a deliberate 100+-point parameter sweep, firmly off-nominal; normal campaign runs use a handful of configs and never approach 99. (2) The finding overstates impact with the incorrect orphaning sub-claim. It is still real silent corruption when the >100-distinct-config edge is hit, so real=true, but it is an edge-path bug, not silent corruption on every valid run. Not a duplicate: the only prior-audit hit on resolve_output_dir (2026-06-25-deep-audit-findings.json:183) is a different defect (datetime YAML values crashing compute_hash). Suggested fix (width-adaptive glob `{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]*`) is correct and the existing `int(dp.name.split('_')[-1])` parse already tolerates 3+ digits.

confirmed.title: hash_exclude_keys ('diagnostics') is incorrectly applied to top-level instrument keys in the dynamic-key branch, so an instrument literally named 'diagnostics' is dropped from the canonical hash
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL but correctly a nit. config_base.py:170-175 dynamic-key branch filters `if k not in self.hash_exclude_keys` where k is an instrument key. perturb/config.py sets hash_exclude_keys=frozenset({'diagnostics'}) and dynamic_key_sections=frozenset({'instruments'}); 'diagnostics' is a per-section toggle param ('diagnostics': False) meant for STATIC sections, not an instrument key. Reproduced on this branch: canonicalize('instruments', {'diagnostics': {'exclude_shear_probes': ['sh1']}}) == canonicalize('instruments', {}) -> {'instruments':{}}, while a normal SN479 key is retained. So an instrument keyed 'diagnostics' vanishes from the hash and could yield a stale output-dir reuse / missed recompute. Practical impact is negligible (correctly nit): instrument keys come from p_path.parent.name (the SN folder, e.g. SN479), so collision requires a data dir literally named 'diagnostics/' plus a matching instruments override — implausible. Even then only the hash drops it: write_resolved_config (config_base.py:298-299) does NOT filter dynamic sections so config.yaml still records the override, and the override is still applied at pipeline.py:1198 (config.get('instruments',{}).get(inst_lookup,{})). No silent corruption of the primary epsilon/chi result; worst case is an output-versioning edge. Not touched by PR #78 (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, ct_align.py, bottom.py); not a duplicate. No test covers this edge. Holds on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. Suggested fix (drop the hash_exclude_keys filter in the dynamic branch) is correct.

confirmed.title: merge_config silently discards ALL overrides for dynamic-key sections (empty {} defaults), losing user data if ever called on 'instruments'
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. ConfigManager.merge_config (config_base.py:137-164) seeds `merged = dict(self.defaults[section])` at line 152 and only retains keys where `k in merged` (lines 156, 161). For a dynamic-key section the defaults dict is empty: perturb/config.py:241 defines `"instruments": {}` and line 250 marks it the sole entry in _DYNAMIC_KEY_SECTIONS. So any override is dropped. Unlike _canonicalize_section (line 170), write_resolved_config (lines 289, 298), and validate_config (line 125), merge_config has no `if section in self.dynamic_key_sections` branch — it is the lone dynamic-section-unaware method, confirming the inconsistency claim. Reproduced live: `merge_config('instruments', {'SN465': {'exclude_shear_probes': ['sh1']}})` returns `{}`, dropping the entire override. The finding's central caveat is also correct and material to severity: this is NOT triggered on any current path. I grepped all ~40 merge_config call sites (rsi/cli.py:182, perturb/pipeline.py, perturb/cli.py) — every one passes a static section (files/gps/hotel/speed/qc/fp07/ct/bottom/top_trim/profiles/epsilon/chi/ctd/netcdf/stratification/binning); none passes 'instruments'. The instruments section is consumed directly via config.get('instruments') (pipeline.py:180, 1198) and _canonical_instruments_for_hash (pipeline.py:133-143) for the diss-dir hash — never through merge_config. Therefore there is no current run on this branch where any real output is corrupted or lost; the defect is purely latent. The defect genuinely holds as described (factually accurate mechanics, accurate latency, accurate inconsistency), so real=true, but its practical impact is zero today, so nit (cosmetic/latent) is the correct and honestly-self-assessed severity. Not fixed by PR #78 (which touches chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py — not config_base.py).

confirmed.title: compute_hash/canonicalize crash on mixed-type dict keys (numeric + string instrument serials, hotel.channels, qc.rules)
confirmed.reasoning: REAL but OVERSTATED — downgraded major -> minor. Every mechanical claim reproduces on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. config_base.py:51 `{str(k): _normalize_nested(val) for k, val in sorted(v.items())}` and :173 `sorted((params or {}).items())` sort dict keys directly; pipeline.py:136 (_canonical_instruments_for_hash) likewise does `sorted((instruments or {}).items())`. ruamel.yaml parses unquoted `465:` as int and `SN479:` as str (verified). I directly reproduced TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'int' from pc.compute_hash("instruments", {465:{...}, "SN479":{...}}), pl._upstream_for("diss", {"instruments": {465:..., "SN479":...}}), pc.compute_hash("hotel", {"channels": {1:..., "T1":...}}) (channels is a real default dict-key), and pc.compute_hash("qc", {"rules": {1:..., "r2":...}}) (rules is a real default dict-key). confirmed.title: Concurrent runs of two different configs against the same output_root can collide into one directory (no lock / no O_EXCL)
confirmed.reasoning: Code matches the finding. resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:240-258) does a non-atomic sequence: glob `base/{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]`, scan each dir for a `.params_sha256_<target_hash>` file returning on first match (lines 251-253), compute next_seq = max_seq+1, then `new_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` (line 257) followed by write_signature (line 258). grep for fcntl|flock|O_EXCL|filelock across config_base.py, rsi/config.py, perturb/config.py returns nothing — no locking exists. The described TOCTOU race is genuine: two processes running different configs against the same base both observe the same max_seq, both select the same `{prefix}_NN`, both mkdir it (exist_ok=True swallows the second's error), and each writes its own signature file, so one directory ends up holding two distinct `.params_sha256_*` signatures plus two configs' co-mingled outputs; a later reuse scan matches by hash and silently associates outputs with a directory containing foreign output. Triggering the race requires a user to manually launch two SEPARATE top-level commands with DIFFERENT configs pointed at the same output_root, overlapping within a sub-second window — an unsupported, unusual usage pattern (different configs are normally run precisely to land in distinct sequence dirs). It is not wrong-on-every-run (not a blocker) and not a corruption on the default single-process path (below the audit's major bar). Original severity "minor" (off-nominal/edge requiring concurrent unsupported invocation) is honest and appropriate; I confirm minor.

confirmed.title: _variance_correction leaks a NumPy RuntimeWarning on degenerate kB=inf (np.trapezoid subtract not inside errstate)

No test exercises the inf path (test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:50 uses only finite kB). Not touched by PR #78 (line 108 still reads `if not (kB > 0)`).

confirmed.title: merge: no time-continuity/restarted check — 29 independent ARCTERX casts fuse into one chain (regressed/unfixed audit #1)
confirmed.reasoning:

PFile-read start times prove these are independent casts, not size-limit rollovers: 0002 starts 2025-01-14 15:30:17 and records 175.7 min (ends ~18:26), then 0003 starts 2025-01-15 14:00:18 — a multi-hour/multi-day gap; 0008 records 36.1 min (ends ~06:09) then 0009 starts 06:12:51 (~4 min gap). confirmed.title: merge: no record-boundary validation of base or continuation files before splicing
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. The mechanism is accurately described: merge_p_files (merge.py:224-235) copies chain[0] in full via shutil.copyfileobj, then for each continuation seeks past header_size+config_size (line 232) and copyfileobj's the remainder to EOF (line 235). There is NO check that (file_size - header_size - config_size) % record_size == 0 for any member, even though _read_merge_info already reads record_size (merge.py:35), so the assert would be cheap. If a non-final chain member carried a fractional trailing record, those bytes would be appended verbatim and shift every subsequent file's records by a sub-record offset; PFile._read computes n_records from chain[0]'s geometry via floor division and only warns about a single TRAILING partial record (p_file.py:349-359), so misaligned interior records are silently mis-sliced with no error. tests/test_perturb_merge.py only exercises integer-record-aligned inputs (f2_data = n_records * record_size), so the gap is uncovered. So the defect is genuine and the suggested fix is valid.\n\nHowever the practical impact is bounded, which keeps it at minor (off-nominal/edge), matching the original severity: (1) In the default pipeline this cannot occur. files.trim defaults to True (config.py:25) and trim runs BEFORE merge (pipeline.py:1774 then 1813); trim_p_file guarantees each output is integer-record aligned by writing only first_record_size + n_complete*record_size bytes (trim.py:148-157). So merge's input is always per-file aligned on the default path. (2) The trigger requires the user to explicitly set files.trim:false (the finding's own premise) AND have a mid-chain file truncated to a fractional record — RSI rollover normally writes complete files except possibly the final one, so a fractional record in a NON-final chain member is rare. (3) merge defaults to False (config.py:27). This is a real latent robustness gap on a non-default, off-nominal config combination, not silent corruption on a normal run. It is not a duplicate of the four PR #78 fixes (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py); PR #78 does not touch merge.py.

confirmed.title: _mle_fit_kB applies the unresolved-variance correction over [0, K_max] but integrates observed variance over [K_fit_low, K_max], biasing chi low for low-epsilon windows
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed on this branch (fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors). chi.py:440-447 calls _variance_correction(kB_best, K_max_fit, speed, tau0, _h2, grad_func) with no K_min argument, so K_min defaults to 0.0 (chi.py:93). _variance_correction integrates V_resolved over the mask (K_fine >= K_min) & (K_fine <= K_max), i.e. effectively [~0, K_max] (chi.py:125-128). Meanwhile obs_var at chi.py:449-451 is trapezoid over K[fit_mask], whose lower edge is the first wavenumber above 2x noise (_valid_wavenumber_mask, chi.py:170-171), i.e. K_fit_low > 0. So V_resolved counts model variance in [0, K_fit_low] that obs_var excludes, inflating V_resolved, shrinking V_total/V_resolved, and under-correcting chi. I verified the bias direction and magnitude numerically: with K_min=0 the correction is stuck at 1.120 regardless of K_fit_low, whereas the correct value with K_min=K_fit_low rises to 1.456 (K_fit_low=5) and 2.701 (K_fit_low=10) for kB=30 cpm — chi is under-estimated by up to ~2.4x. The fraction of model variance below K_fit_low for kB=30 is 21%/54% at K_fit_low=5/10 cpm, versus <0.1% for kB=200 at K_fit_low=1-2 cpm, so the bug bites only low-epsilon/low-signal windows. The _iterative_fit default path correctly passes K_min=k_l_new (chi.py:588-589), and _chi_from_epsilon's use of the default correction (chi.py:261) only seeds the grid centre for a subsequent log-LSQ fit, so it is harmless. Severity is correctly minor, not major: fit_method defaults to 'iterative' in every entry point (rsi/config.py:53, rsi/chi_io.py:45/230, rsi/pipeline.py:108, perturb/config.py:118), so no default run is affected; the defect is reached only via the explicitly opt-in fit_method='mle' path (chi/l4_chi.py:226,259), where it silently under-estimates chi in low-signal windows. No test pins the mle correction band, so it is genuinely uncaught. Note: the finding's path 'l4_chi.py' lives at chi/l4_chi.py, not scor160/l4_chi.py, but that citation slip does not affect the substance. PR #78's grid change did not introduce this; the missing K_min is pre-existing.

confirmed.title: pair_nearest auto-tolerance collapses to 0 when >50% of source-time spacings are zero (degenerate/duplicate src_times)
confirmed.reasoning: The cited mechanism is real and I reproduced it. At mixing.py:511-514, when max_dt is None and len(src_times) > 1, the tolerance is set to float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(src_times)))). If more than half of consecutive sorted source-time diffs are 0 (duplicated source times), the median is exactly 0.0, so `ok = dt <= max_dt` (line 528) becomes `dt <= 0` and only destinations landing exactly on a source time are paired; all others get NaN. Confirmed: pair_nearest([5,5,5],[1,2,3],[5.0]) -> [1.] but pair_nearest([5,5,5],[1,2,3],[5.1]) -> [nan] (median diff 0.0), so a destination 0.1s from a source is dropped. HOWEVER, this is NOT triggered on any valid run on this branch. The two production call sites pass strictly-increasing time vectors: (1) rsi/pipeline.py:462 passes l4.time, which is l3.time (l4.py:234) built from l2.time[center_idx] with center_idx = sec_start + w*diss_step + diss_length//2 and diss_step >= 1 over a monotone time vector (l3.py:124,174,176) — strictly increasing within and across temporally-ordered sections; (2) perturb/pipeline.py:764 passes epoch seconds of diss_ds['t'] (epsilon bin/profile times), also monotonic. The finding itself concedes 'In production l4.time are strictly increasing window centres so this is latent, not currently triggered.' No CLI or external caller feeds arbitrary/duplicate src_times within the repo (it is a public export of the processing package, so third-party misuse is theoretically possible, but not exercised here). The data-loss outcome (NaN'd Gamma/K_rho) only arises from input the pipeline structurally cannot produce. The existing len==1 -> 30.0 fallback shows the author intended a sensible default, so guarding a non-positive median is a reasonable latent-robustness improvement, but the original 'nit' severity is honest and correctly calibrated: latent edge-case, no impact on the primary result on any valid run on this branch, not fixed/affected by PR #78 (unrelated to chi/profile/ct_align/bottom).

confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code; all claims hold. confirmed.reasoning: The mechanism is real and verified. ocean.py:122 declares `lat: float = 0` and line 148 passes it to `gsw.Nsquared(SA, CT, P, lat)`. gsw.Nsquared scales N^2 by latitude-dependent gravity, so a caller omitting lat gets equatorial-gravity N^2. The finding's claim that the production path threads a real lat is also correct: perturb/pipeline.py:618-620 extracts `lat` from the profile and pipeline.py:632 passes it through sorted_stratification -> profile_stratification -> _stable_window -> gsw.Nsquared. However, the practical impact on this branch is essentially nil, which warrants downgrading minor -> nit: (1) grep confirms buoyancy_freq has ZERO callers in src/ (only tests/test_epsilon.py and tests/test_scor160_ocean.py) — no production output is biased by it; (2) even hypothetically, at the actual ARCTERX/Saipan latitude (~15N) the bias is 0.07%, far below N^2 estimation uncertainty; (3) the finding itself states "impact is latent." So the defect genuinely exists (a true latent code-quality issue / convenience-function footgun) but it is cosmetic/latent on this branch rather than off-nominal behavior, mapping to nit under the rubric. confirmed.title: buoyancy_freq produces inf/nan N^2 on duplicate/non-monotonic input pressure (unguarded dp=0 division)
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED REAL but recalibrated minor -> nit (purely latent, no production impact). The technical claim holds against current branch code: ocean.py:148 `N2, p_mid = gsw.Nsquared(SA, CT, P, lat)` passes P straight through with no monotonicity/spacing guard. Reproduced on the installed gsw 3.6.21 via the public API: with P=[0,50,50,100] and differing T across the duplicate pressure, `buoyancy_freq` returns N2=[9.83e-05, inf, 8.48e-05] (inf, silent, no exception, no warning surfaced to the caller); when dT=0 at the duplicate it yields nan instead (0/0). Either way a non-finite N^2 silently propagates. The finding's contrast claim is also correct: the production path in processing/mixing.py guards this with `if p_pair[1]-p_pair[0] < min_dp/2: return nan` at lines 198 and 314 — but crucially those paths call gsw.Nsquared DIRECTLY and never route through buoyancy_freq, so they are independently protected and unaffected. I confirmed there are ZERO src/ callers of buoyancy_freq (only tests/test_scor160_ocean.py and tests/test_epsilon.py reference it) and it is not re-exported in any __init__.py. Thus no production output on any real run can be corrupted — the defect is reachable only by a direct external/notebook import feeding binned/decimated pressures. PR #78's four fixes (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py) do not touch ocean.py, so this is neither fixed nor introduced by it. Because the only impact is a robustness/precondition gap on an exported helper with no current caller able to trigger it, the honest severity is nit (latent), not minor (which the rubric reserves for off-nominal/edge paths that are actually reachable). The suggested fix (validate strictly monotonic P / floor on adjacent dp, or document the precondition) is reasonable.

confirmed.title: ct_align discards an entire profile's lag contribution on a single non-finite C/T sample (over-discard)
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against the current code on this branch. ct_align.py:83-84 reads `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue`, which does drop the ENTIRE profile segment when any single sample is non-finite — the over-discard the finding describes. The premise is also factually correct: convert_jac_c (channels.py:237-238) emits NaN only for isolated corrupt samples via `np.where(v_part == 0, np.nan, i_part/v_part)`. The IIR lfilter at lines 86-87 genuinely propagates a single NaN across the whole segment, so this skip is the only way to avoid poisoning, but it forfeits an otherwise-usable multi-thousand-sample profile; the suggested per-sample mask/interpolate-before-diff fix is technically sound. The fallback-to-no-alignment claim is real but the path actually reached when all profiles are skipped is the empty-`per_profile` guard at line 119-120 (`return C.copy(), 0.0`), not the `total<=0` path at line 130 the finding cites — same observable outcome (no shift applied), minor mis-citation. Severity stays minor and is NOT overstated: the finding explicitly acknowledges the skip is correct versus the pre-fix poisoning and only claims it is coarser than necessary. Triggering requires v_part==0 hardware/transmission corruption (rare), and total collapse to zero-alignment requires essentially every profile in a cast to carry a NaN; it degrades a secondary CT-alignment correction (the lag is returned and observable), not the primary epsilon/chi result, and causes no silent data loss. Genuine off-nominal/edge robustness improvement, correctly classified as minor. Not fixed by PR #78 — PR #78 introduced this very guard; this is a refinement of it, and the M3 test (test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:114) only covers the single-NaN-profile case, not the all-profiles-corrupt over-discard.

confirmed.title: _load_from_nc leaks the open NetCDF Dataset handle on every error/exception path
confirmed.reasoning: VERIFIED against current code on branch fix/audit-2026-06-25-majors. The defect is factually accurate: `ds = nc.Dataset(str(nc_path), "r")` is opened at line 349, and `ds.close()` is only invoked at line 458 on the success path. There is no try/finally or `with` block protecting the span. Any exception raised in between leaks the handle: the explicit `raise ValueError` at lines 385, 416, 418 (missing time/pressure variables), the `AttributeError` from `_get_nc_attr` at line 370 (missing fs_fast/fs_slow), or a KeyError from `g.variables["TIME"]`/`["TIME_SLOW"]` at lines 382-383/397-401 when L1_converted lacks expected vars. Practical impact is small and latent: (1) it is only on the `.nc` re-extraction path reached via `_load_source` line 279, not the default `.p` pipeline path (`_load_from_pfile`); (2) the leak only fires on the ERROR path when the NetCDF file is malformed/missing expected variables — the normal success path closes correctly; (3) the descriptor is reclaimed at process exit, so descriptor exhaustion requires many malformed `.nc` files processed in one long-lived process, an unusual scenario; (4) no silent output corruption, no wrong primary result, no default-path crash. confirmed.title: Dead `n_fine`/`_N_FINE` parameter in _variance_correction after the kB-scaled grid rewrite
confirmed.reasoning: Verified against current branch code. chi.py:113 computes `n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB)`. With _K_SPAN_KB=40 (line 69) and _PTS_PER_KB=2000 (line 70), the floor is 40*2000 = 80000. The only n_fine value ever supplied is the default n_fine=_N_FINE=2000 (lines 62, 92): a repo-wide grep confirms n_fine/_N_FINE appear ONLY inside chi.py (definition, signature, docstring, max() call) and nowhere in tests. All three call sites — line 261 (`_variance_correction(kB, K_max, speed, tau0, _h2, grad_func)`), lines 440-447, and lines 588-589 — pass no n_fine argument, so it is always 2000 and max(2000, 80000)=80000 unconditionally. This is real but purely a maintainability/dead-parameter issue: no numerical result changes, no crash, no data loss — the 80000-point kB-scaled grid is exactly what the rewrite intends, and the docstring (line 111) already notes n_fine is 'a floor'.
