refuted.title: Variance-method polyfit window forced to >=7 cpm where ODAS allows down to K_95 (lower bound 0), introducing noise-floor bins into the spectral-minimum fit for very-low-epsilon windows
refuted.reasoning: The factual port divergence is REAL and accurately cited: l4.py:479 computes the polyfit-window upper bound as `min(max(min(K_AA,K_95), K_LIMIT_MIN=7), K_LIMIT_MAX=150)` (lower clamp 7), whereas ODAS get_diss_odas.m:563 uses `find(K <= fit_in_range(min([K_AA,K_95]),[0 150]))`, and fit_in_range (def at get_diss_odas.m:740-753) clamps to [0,150] (lower bound 0). I confirmed K_95 = X_95*(e/nu^3)^0.25 with X_95=0.1205 (matches ODAS x_95) gives K_95<7 only for e < ~2.7e-11 W/kg (e=1e-11 -> 5.41, e=1e-12 -> 3.04), so the divergence exists only in that low-epsilon regime — the finding's numbers check out. HOWEVER the claimed practical consequence ("change the detected K_limit_log... change n_var / integrated variance range") does NOT hold. In exactly the regime where the window divergence exists, min(K_AA,K_95) < 7, and the final integration limit is unconditionally pinned to 7 cpm in BOTH implementations: (1) any polyfit-derived spectral minimum must be >= log10(10) cpm (l4.py:503 / ODAS:595); (2) l4.py:510 then clamps `K_limit_log = min(K_limit_log, log10(K_95), log10(K_AA))`, and since K_95<7<10 this forces K_limit_log down to log10(K_95) whether or not a root was detected — the detected minimum is entirely discarded; (3) l4.py:511 clamps up to max(.., log10(7)), yielding exactly 7 cpm. I traced this numerically: for e in {1e-12,1e-11,2e-11}, final K_limit = 7.000 cpm with root_found True or False. ODAS does the identical [log10(7),log10(150)] clamp at lines 605-606 and the same 7-cpm Range bump at 608-610 (mirrored by l4.py:517-518). When min(K_AA,K_95) >= 7 the two window expressions are bit-identical (lower clamp inactive in both). Therefore the divergent polyfit window changes only an intermediate detection value that is provably washed out before it can affect n_var, e_3, or the final epsilon — there is no execution path on this branch where the output differs. The finding's own evidence concedes "the integrated variance range is identical." This is a latent/cosmetic divergence with zero output impact, not a defect that matters in practice. Recalibrated to not-a-bug. No test gap is materially exposed since behavior is output-identical to ODAS.

refuted.title: process_l4 EPSI_FINAL fallback geometric-mean includes probes flagged for inter-probe inconsistency (bit 4) and FOM (bit 1), reporting a 'final' epsilon for windows where all probes failed QC
refuted.reasoning:
The cited code matches exactly: _compute_epsi_final (l4.py:766-779) tries probes with flags==0 (line 771), and when none pass, falls back to the geometric mean of all finite positive probes regardless of flag bits (lines 774-778). So the mechanism described is accurate. However the finding is REFUTED on its central claim and on materiality:

(1) The finding's core complaint — "no flag is propagated onto epsi_final itself... indistinguishable downstream from a clean estimate" — is factually wrong. epsi_flags is computed (l4.py:217-230) and exported on the L4Data object right beside epsi_final (l4.py:238-240; declared in io.py:196-197 and round-tripped in io.py:664-679). Every downstream consumer (pipeline.py, l4_chi.py, compare.py) has the full per-probe QC bitfield available to detect an all-flagged window. The information needed to distinguish a clean EPSI_FINAL from an all-flagged fallback IS propagated; it is simply at per-probe granularity rather than as a separate epsi_final scalar.

(2) The fallback is a deliberate, documented, and explicitly TESTED design choice, not a latent defect. test_scor160_l4.py:231-237 (test_all_flagged_fallback) pins exactly this behavior ("If all probes flagged, fall back to geometric mean of all finite"). The module docstring (l4.py:10) and inline comment (l4.py:775) frame it as the intended ATOMIX combine convention.

(3) Port/benchmark fidelity: the scor160 benchmark validation (compare.py:332-348) compares the computed EPSI_FINAL against the ATOMIX reference's own EPSI_FINAL (ref_ef). The fallback existing and the benchmark gate passing indicates the behavior is consistent with the reference convention rather than a divergence; per project memory, this benchmark is the pinning gate for epsilon changes.

(4) This branch's PR #78 does not touch l4.py (its 4 fixes target chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py). The function is unchanged since the original scor160 commit, so the finding is not introduced or affected by #78.

The only legitimate kernel — there is no single per-window epsi_final quality scalar, and the off-nominal all-flagged fallback averages in probes flagged for inconsistency — is a real but extremely minor design observation that applies only when every probe in a window already failed QC (an off-nominal, fully-flagged case). It is intentional, tested, benchmark-aligned, and the QC information is in fact exported. It is not silent corruption of any primary result. Combined with the finding's incorrect flag-propagation premise and the author's own low confidence / nit rating, this does not represent a bug on this branch.

refuted.title: num_ffts can go negative when diss_length < fft_length, silently disabling FM/dof_spec QC with no validation
refuted.reasoning:
The finding's arithmetic premise is correct (num_ffts = 2*(diss_length//fft_length)-1 = -1 for diss_length=512, fft_length=1024), but its documented impact does not occur on this branch, so the claimed defect ("the spectra still compute, so the user gets epsilon estimates with QC silently degraded") is false.

Key facts read from the current code:
1. Default path is safe: dissipation.py:101-102 and pipeline.py:145-146 set diss_length = 4*fft_length when unset, giving num_ffts = 7 > 0. So no default run is affected.
2. The ONLY way num_ffts <= 0 is diss_length < fft_length. But in that exact regime the spectra never compute: process_l3 (called at dissipation.py:188 and pipeline.py:339) invokes csd_matrix_batch with the FFT-segment overlap hardcoded to nfft//2 (l3.py:148). In csd_matrix_batch (scor160/spectral.py:371-375) n_seg = (diss_length - overlap)//step; for diss_length<fft_length this yields n_seg == 0, which raises ValueError("diss_length (...) too short for nfft=..."). I verified by brute force over fft_length in {256,512,1024} that the smallest diss_length giving n_seg>=1 is exactly diss_length==fft_length, where num_ffts==1; throughout the entire num_ffts<=0 region n_seg==0. So {spectra compute} and {num_ffts<=0} are mutually exclusive.
3. There is no try/except around process_l3 in either dissipation.py (line 188) or pipeline.py (line 339), so the ValueError propagates as a clear, descriptive hard error BEFORE num_ffts (dissipation.py:198 / pipeline.py:352) is ever computed and before process_l4's `num_ffts > 0` QC branch (l4.py:216,428) is reached.

Consequence: the misconfiguration is already loudly rejected with a hard error pointing at the too-short window; it does not silently produce epsilon with degraded QC. The num_ffts<=0 -> disabled-FM path the finding describes is unreachable via this route. PR #78 does not touch this; it is not-a-bug on this branch (at most the error message names nfft rather than the QC consequence, which is cosmetic).

refuted.title: fom variance-ratio QC flag (bit 1) does not catch negative observed variance after Goodman cleaning
refuted.reasoning:
The finding's central premise is structurally false on this branch. It claims that after Goodman cleaning, sh_spec_clean bins can be negative, so obs_var = np.trapezoid(spec_safe_pos, K_pos) (l4.py:411) and hence fom_val (l4.py:413) can be negative, slipping past the `fom > fom_limit` flag (l4.py:731). But the cleaned shear spectra that reach _estimate_epsilon are explicitly floored to be non-negative.

Trace: l3.process_l3 builds sh_freq_spec_clean from np.diagonal(clean_UU, ...) (l3.py:164-166) — the auto-spectra diagonal. clean_UU comes from clean_shear_spec_batch, which calls _sanitize_autospectra (goodman.py:267). _sanitize_autospectra (goodman.py:36-42) sets each diagonal bin to np.where(diag_i < 0, 0.0, diag_i), i.e. floors negative over-subtracted auto-spectra to 0 (its docstring at goodman.py:27-31 explicitly states this is to handle Goodman over-subtraction driving the cleaned auto-spectrum negative). The single-window clean_shear_spec path likewise sanitizes (goodman.py:201). Downstream scalings are all positivity-preserving: sh_k_clean *= W with W = max(..., 0.05) > 0 (l3.py:212,220), Macoun-Lueck ml_corr >= 1 (l3.py:57-59,225), and the bias correction is a positive count ratio (goodman.py:271).

Thus spec_safe in _estimate_epsilon contains only non-negative bins (NaN bins are handled separately by the interpolate/invalidate logic at l4.py:347-355). A trapezoid integral of non-negative values over a monotone-increasing K is >= 0 (verified empirically), so obs_var and fom_val cannot be negative. The only edge is obs_var == 0 (all-zero integration band), but l4.py:186 already skips windows with no positive finite bins, and a fom==0 degenerate window would be caught by the var_resolved (bit 16) and inter-probe consistency (bit 4) flags — and is not the negative-fom mechanism the finding describes.

The finding correctly notes that fom_for_flags = fom when num_ffts==0 (l4.py:216) and that this path is the default for direct process_l4 calls (num_ffts default 0, l4.py:94) — but the consequence it draws (a negative fom evading bit 1) cannot occur because the precondition (negative cleaned spectrum) is prevented by _sanitize_autospectra. This protection is pre-existing and independent of PR #78. Not a real defect.

refuted.title: _nc_filled makes the days->seconds time-axis NaN-fragile when TIME carries a _FillValue
refuted.reasoning:
Read profile.py:330-456 and convert.py:122-141,190-209,450-531. The core mechanism is partly real: _nc_filled (line 342, np.ma.filled(...,np.nan)) turns a masked TIME element into NaN, and line 453 `t_fast=(t_fast-t_fast[0])*86400.0` propagates it. I empirically confirmed that if the FIRST TIME element is a _FillValue, the entire vector becomes NaN. HOWEVER the finding is overstated and does not matter in practice on this branch:

1) NO-OP on every real run. The finding itself concedes package-written L1 has no _FillValue on TIME. Confirmed: convert.py writes TIME via _create_l1_variables (createVariable WITHOUT fill_value, then v[:]=full array), so all elements are written and netCDF4 returns an unmasked array (verified empirically: _nc_filled yields no NaN). _load_from_nc only ever consumes package-produced full-record .nc files (rsi-tpw nc -> rsi-tpw prof); no current pipeline or test feeds an external CF file with a masked TIME element.

2) The documented external/benchmark path does NOT route through this code. ATOMIX/AtomixData *.nc are read by scor160/io.py and rsi/adapter.py via g.variables['TIME'][:], not _load_from_nc.

3) The 'interior NaN breaks np.interp -> all-NaN' claim is FALSE/overstated. I verified: an interior fill NaNs only that one element of (t-t[0]); np.interp against an xp with a single interior NaN returns mostly-finite (locally wrong) values, NOT all-NaN. The all-NaN catastrophe requires the FIRST element specifically to be fill.

4) Mis-attributed as 'PR #78 M2 collateral'. The (t-t[0]) line is pre-existing (verified at parent commit dde72a8, where time was read via [:].data). Pre-#78 the same contrived external file produced raw-fill garbage (~9.97e36 finite) which is arguably worse; PR #78 only changed the symptom, it did not introduce a defect.

Triggering requires a contrived non-package CF file with an L1_converted group, 'Days' units, and a masked _FillValue specifically in the TIME coordinate variable — pathological for a CF time axis. It is a genuine latent robustness gap (t_fast[0] is not NaN-robust) but affects no valid run on this branch, so real=false and severity downgraded minor->nit.

refuted.title: PFile uses np.fromfile(count=n_records) from a prior file-size stat — concurrent truncation yields a silent short read then a cryptic reshape error
refuted.reasoning: The mechanism is technically accurate and I reproduced it: p_file.py:348 stats file_size, :349 computes n_records, :376 does np.fromfile(..., count=n_records), :378 computes total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record from the (now stale) n_records, and :379 reshapes records['data'] to total_scans. I confirmed empirically that np.fromfile with a structured dtype silently returns fewer records than requested on a short file (asked 10, got 3) and that the subsequent reshape raises 'cannot reshape array of size 300 into shape (10,100)'. However, this does not represent a real defect that matters on this branch: (1) It is NOT silent corruption or data loss — the outcome is a loud ValueError, so the process fails rather than emitting bad data. (2) It requires a concurrent truncation of the .p file in the microsecond window between the stat (:348) and the read (:376). The documented workflow post-processes COMPLETED .p recordings; I grepped the codebase and found nothing that truncates or rewrites a .p file while PFile reads it (config_patch.py and p_file.py:180-181 writes target separate output files, not the file under read). So the cited failure path is effectively unreachable under real usage. (3) The realistic truncation case — a file that is short at stat time (interrupted download / partial DAQ) — is ALREADY correctly handled: n_records is derived from the live file_size, n_records<1 raises a clear error at :350-351, and a non-integer record count warns at :352-359 (mirroring read_odas.m:184-187). The finding itself rates severity=nit, confidence=low, and scopes it as 'narrow (requires concurrent truncation)'. The suggested fix (assert len(records)==n_records for a clearer short-read message) is a reasonable defensive nicety but addresses only error-message quality in a TOCTOU race that the codebase never triggers; there is no actual defect, silent corruption, or unhandled realistic path on this branch.

refuted.title: ct_align silently applies zero CT alignment when sporadic NaN conductivity poisons every profile (PR #78 guard regression)
refuted.reasoning:
I read ct_align.py on this branch and the pre-guard version at 1a78fca^. The finding's structural description is accurate: the line-83 guard `if not (np.all(isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(isfinite(seg_C))): continue` is all-or-nothing, and when every profile is skipped the function returns `C.copy(), 0.0` (lines 119-120 / 130-132) with no log, while the caller (pipeline.py:1049-1057) only logs on exception, not on this normal return. That logging gap is the one true kernel. But three things refute the finding as stated, and the severity is overstated.

(1) Empirical premise is false. The finding claims JAC conductivity "commonly has isolated NaN spikes" and "every profile carries one." convert_jac_c (channels.py:238) only emits NaN when v_part==0 (a genuinely corrupt zero-voltage sample). I scanned all 29 readable VMP/*.p files: ZERO non-finite conductivity samples total. The all-skipped fallback is therefore essentially unreachable on the real dataset; it would require every profile in a file to contain a corrupt sample.

(2) The "regresses discoverability vs pre-guard, which at least produced some lag estimate" claim is wrong. I read 1a78fca^: pre-guard used `if norm <= 0:`, which is False for NaN, so a NaN-poisoned profile slipped through to corr/norm (all-NaN) and argmax(|NaN|)=0 -> lag = -max_lag_seconds, a maximally-wrong value that poisoned the weighted median. The guard is a strict correctness improvement, not a regression. test_nan_profile_does_not_drive_minus_max_lag (test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:114) locks this in: a NaN in one profile no longer drags the consensus; clean profiles correctly carry it.

(3) Impact is over-classified as "silent data degradation." The consequence is the absence of a sub-second CT lag shift (median_lag is typically a fraction of a second), not corrupted or lost output; the CTD product still ships with raw (un-aligned) C. Moreover the caller discards `_lag` (pipeline.py:1049) and never logs the applied lag on ANY path, so there is no per-run lag diagnostic to "regress" from. The redundant `not np.isfinite(norm)` check at line 91 is a second net catching the same poison.

Net: a missing-log nit on a path that requires all-profiles-corrupt (not present in real data), with the headline framing partially incorrect. Not silent corruption, not minor — a latent nit. real=false.

refuted.title: pair_nearest aligns epsilon onto chi grid by time only, discarding Gamma/K_rho when the nearest epsilon window is a NaN-rejected estimate
refuted.reasoning:
The mechanical claim is TRUE and reproduces: pair_nearest (mixing.py:522-529) computes `pick` as the temporally nearest source index without checking finiteness, then `out[ok] = sv[pick[ok]]`, so a destination co-located with a NaN source returns NaN even when a finite source sits within max_dt. The demonstrated case src_t=[0,8,16], src_v=[1e-7,nan,3e-7], dst=[8] returns [nan]. And l4.epsi_final IS NaN for fully-QC-rejected windows (_compute_epsi_final, l4.py:766-779 returns np.nan when no probe is good/finite), and mixing_coefficients masks Gamma/K_rho to NaN when eps_ok is false (mixing.py:481-486). So the data-flow premise holds.

However, this is a defensible-by-design behavior, not a defect that matters in practice:

1) In the DEFAULT rsi pipeline, epsilon (l3.py) and chi (l3_chi.py) use identical diss_length and diss_step (both default 4*fft_length and diss_length//2; rsi/pipeline.py:145-160 even warns when they differ), producing 1:1-aligned window grids. The 'valid epsilon at the adjacent window' the finding wants to recover is therefore a DIFFERENT depth/time window. Substituting it to compute Gamma = N2*chi/(2*epsilon*dTdz^2) would pair chi(window j) with epsilon(window j±1) — a physically inconsistent cross-window value mislabeled as window j. The suggested fix ('prefer nearest FINITE source') thus introduces a correctness concern rather than clearly improving correctness. The Osborn ratio needs epsilon co-located with chi.

2) The asymmetric NaN behavior (K_T chi-only survives; Gamma/K_rho fall to NaN when epsilon absent) is an EXPLICITLY DOCUMENTED design choice, not a silent oversight: rsi/pipeline.py:455-457 states '...with no shear epsilon K_T = chi/(2 dT/dz^2) stays valid while Gamma/K_rho fall to NaN (bug_003).' This directly contradicts the finding's 'silent data loss' category.

3) The windows affected are precisely those where epsilon QC already failed — the population where fabricating an epsilon from a neighbor is least appropriate. No measured output is corrupted or lost; a derived diagnostic is conservatively withheld.

PR #78 does not touch mixing.py and is irrelevant here. Original severity 'minor / silent data loss' overstates a conservative, documented design decision whose proposed remedy is arguably less correct. Recalibrated to not-a-bug; real=false.

refuted.title: Iterative/MLE chi fit discards a valid earlier kB when a later refinement step destabilizes to NaN, silently dropping the whole window
refuted.reasoning:
REFUTED. The finding's core premise — that iteration 1 of `_iterative_fit` (or the l4_chi MLE loop) can destabilize to kB=NaN or kB<1 after `chi_obs` is refined, overwriting a valid iteration-0 kB — is not reachable from real inputs. Three independent mechanisms block it:

(1) NaN-via-too-few-points is chi-independent and identical across iterations. `_mle_find_kB` (chi.py:343) computes `fit_mask = _valid_wavenumber_mask(spec_obs, noise_K, K, K_AA, min_points=6)`. None of those inputs depend on `chi_obs`; only `chi_obs` changes between iterations and it is never passed to `_valid_wavenumber_mask`. So the `np.sum(fit_mask) < 6` -> NaN return (line 345) is deterministic across all iterations — if iteration 0 passed it, iteration 1 passes it too.

(2) kB<1 is structurally impossible from the grid. `_KB_COARSE = np.logspace(0, 4.5, 100)` (chi.py:71) starts at 1.0, and the fine grid lower bound is `kB_lo = max(best_coarse*0.5, 1.0)` (line 365). So a non-NaN return is always >= 1.0. The `kB_best < 1` half of the guard at line 557 cannot fire for a successful fit.

(3) NaN-via-all-infinite-NLL cannot occur for finite-positive chi. The only chi-dependent NaN path is line 359 `if np.all(np.isinf(nll_coarse))`. `spec_models = grad_func(...)*H2 + noise` is floored at 1e-30 (line 355), and crucially `batchelor_grad` (batchelor.py:123) and `kraichnan_grad` (batchelor.py:178) BOTH return `np.where(np.isfinite(S), S, 0.0)` — non-finite model values are zeroed. Thus `spec_models` is always finite/positive, `log(spec_models)` and `spec_fit/spec_models` are finite, and `nll_coarse` is never all-inf. The refined `chi_obs` is also always finite-positive: `_variance_correction` returns a finite ratio or NaN (never inf), and the NaN case falls back to `chi_obs = chi_band` or `1e-14` (chi.py:591-596).

Empirical confirmation: feeding `_mle_find_kB` a synthetic valid spectrum with `chi_obs` swept across 17 orders of magnitude (1e-14 to 1e3) returned a finite kB >= 1 in every case — the fit never destabilizes to NaN/<1 from a refined chi.

The l4_chi.py loop uses the same `_mle_fit_kB`/`_mle_find_kB`, and its guard at line 265 already requires `result.kB` finite AND `result.chi` finite AND `result.chi > 0` before feeding back, while `_mle_fit_kB` returns finite-positive chi whenever kB is finite (chi.py:452/454). So the same three protections apply.

The finding's only 'demonstration' was monkeypatching `_mle_find_kB`/`_mle_fit_kB` to return valid-then-NaN — that merely shows the break/overwrite code path executes if you force the helper to lie; it says nothing about real-world reachability. There is no realistic on-branch input that produces the claimed silent window loss. Not a bug.

Read the cited code at l4.py:766-779 (`_compute_epsi_final`). 1) INTENTIONAL AND UNIT-TESTED. tests/test_scor160_l4.py:231-237 `test_all_flagged_fallback` explicitly constructs an all-flagged column (flags=[[1],[1]]) and asserts EPSI_FINAL == exp(mean(log(all finite))). 2) NEVER EXERCISED BY THE BENCHMARK. The finding itself states (and I confirmed against the docs) that no ATOMIX dataset — VMP250 included — contains an all-probes-flagged window, so this path never runs on the reference data and there is zero benchmark evidence it produces a wrong primary result. The EPSI_FINAL comparison in compare.py would not change.

3) INFORMATION IS NOT LOST DOWNSTREAM. The core premise — that the fallback value is 'indistinguishable downstream from a clean estimate' — is overstated. `epsi_flags` (full per-probe QC flag array, N_SHEAR×N_SPECTRA) is carried on L4Data (io.py:196-197) and written/read to/from the output NetCDF as EPSI_FLAGS (io.py:664-679). Any consumer can inspect EPSI_FLAGS to detect a window where every probe was rejected; the QC backing is fully preserved, not silently dropped.

4) DUPLICATE. This element [40] is a strict subset of element [4] in the same round1_2_raw array, which describes the identical `_compute_epsi_final` fallback at the same lines (771/776-778) with more detail (bit-4 inconsistency case).

5) AUTHOR HEDGES. Self-rated nit, confidence 'low', evidence explicitly says 'it may be an intentional always-produce-a-number policy.' It is a documentation/policy-preference suggestion, not a correctness defect. Not fixed by nor targeting PR #78. Verdict: not-a-bug.

refuted.title: Degenerate/duplicate epsilon window times collapse max_dt to 0, silently dropping all Method-1 chi
refuted.reasoning: The cited code matches: l4_chi.py:170 computes `max_dt = float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(epsi_times)))) if len(epsi_times) > 1 else 30.0`, and lines 174-176 reject any chi window with |epsi_times[idx_eps]-chi_times[j]| > max_dt. So a max_dt of exactly 0.0 WOULD NaN all but bit-exact-matching Method-1 chi. However, the precondition cannot arise in practice. For np.median(np.diff(np.sort(...))) to equal 0.0, a strict MAJORITY of consecutive sorted epsi_times diffs must be exactly zero — i.e. the timestamp array must be dominated by bit-identical duplicates. The finding's reproducer [100,100,100] is an all-identical array; I confirmed numerically that a normal monotonic window grid (np.arange(0,30,2)) and a two-profile concat both yield median diff 2.0, not 0.0. epsi_times come from epsilon_ds.coords['t'] (chi_io.py:382), which are distinct monotonic spectral-window centers spaced by diss_length within a profile. The finding's claimed source — the load_epsilon_dataset 'double-concat' (chi_io.py:857-871) — does not produce duplicates: each distinct per-profile file is loaded exactly once (the single `{stem}_eps.nc` does not match the `{stem}_prof*_eps.nc` glob, so no self-duplication), and concat stitches together DISTINCT profiles taken at DISTINCT wall-clock times. There is no code path on this branch that yields a majority of bit-identical epsilon timestamps; a few accidental duplicates would not zero the median. The finding was already filed as nit/edge-case/medium-confidence, and since its triggering condition cannot occur with real input the practical impact is nil. At most this is a latent hardening opportunity (floor max_dt above 0), not a bug.

refuted.title: Duplicate time-bin coalescing in make_combo silently drops one file's data and is non-deterministic under np.argsort's default (non-stable) quicksort
refuted.reasoning:
The code at the cited lines matches the finding: combo.py:110 `order = np.argsort(tk)` (default non-stable quicksort), :118 `keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]` (keep-first on exact duplicates), :136 `combo = combo.isel(time=np.flatnonzero(keep))` (drops duplicate rows). But the headline claim is REFUTED on two counts.

(1) "Silently drops" is false. combo.py:128-135 emits `logger.warning("make_combo(time): dropped %d duplicate and %d non-finite timestamp(s)...")`, and tests/test_perturb_combo.py:`test_time_combo_logs_dropped_duplicate_count` asserts that exact log message ("dropped 2 duplicate and 1 non-finite"). The keep-first dedup is an intentional, comment-documented design for CF strict monotonicity (issues #21/#51) with a passing dedicated test `test_time_combo_overlapping_ranges_strictly_monotonic`. So it is neither silent nor unintended.

(2) The non-stable-argsort non-determinism is a real latent property but practically inert. It only manifests when two rows share an EXACT float-bit-equal timestamp. Per ctd.py:312-317 each CTD file builds its own bin grid `np.arange(t_lo, t_hi+bw, bw)` starting at that file's own `t_lo=nanmin(pf.t_slow)` (file-relative, ~0; p_file.py:475-476), and ctd.py:347 sets `time_epoch = bin_centers + pf.start_time.timestamp()` (each file's own absolute epoch offset). Exact float equality of two independently computed `t_lo + k*bw + epoch_offset` sums across files essentially never occurs in real data — the finding itself rates impact "low." Even if a duplicate occurred, the non-stable order only changes WHICH of two same-time rows survives; the tests assert only monotonicity and drop-counts (order-invariant), so no flakiness, and I confirmed all 5 relevant tests pass.

Net: the load-bearing "silent data loss" claim is wrong (logged + tested), and the residual determinism concern is a trivial latent hardening (`kind="stable"`) gated behind an essentially-impossible precondition in this pipeline. This is a cosmetic/latent nit, not a defect that matters in practice on this branch — real=false.

refuted.title: despike.py raises bare ValueError (no guard) for inputs of length <= 2
refuted.reasoning:
Behavioral claim is empirically TRUE but it is not a defect that matters on this branch. Verified by direct execution: despike() with length 1 or 2 raises `ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6` from scipy filtfilt; length >= 3 works. The line citation is slightly off: the finding says "109-110", but on this branch line 109 is the `butter(...)` call and the filtfilt that actually raises is line 110.

Why this does not matter in practice:
1. UNREACHABLE INPUT. I traced every caller via grep. Every one feeds real instrument time series, never a 1-2-sample array: scor160/l2.py (despike of descent-section slices at ~512 Hz, thousands of samples), chi/l2_chi.py:105 (per-section fast-temperature slices temp_fast[ci, mask]), rsi/viewer_base.py:580 (profile segments), perturb/plot/diagnostics.py:132 (pressure-bin fast-channel slices). A "section" in this domain is a profile/multi-second fast-sampled window. A 1-2-sample segment is not a reachable state in any default or documented run; the orchestrators do not slice data down to 1-2 samples.
2. DELIBERATE EXISTING CONTRACT. tests/test_edge_cases.py::test_empty_array (line 53-55) explicitly asserts `despike([])` RAISES ValueError, and test_short_array (line 57-60) tests length-3 (works). So the codebase already treats "ValueError on degenerate input" as the accepted, tested behavior; the empty case is intentionally unguarded. Length 1-2 is the same degenerate-input regime, just with no dedicated test.
3. The finding's own evidence concedes a degenerate 1-2-sample segment "carries no useful information so erroring is defensible."
4. Not touched by PR #78 (its 4 fixes are in chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py, not despike.py), so PR #78 neither introduces nor fixes this.

Net: it is a real behavioral observation but produces no silent corruption, no data loss, and no crash on any valid/reachable run; it is an unhelpful error message for an input that cannot occur. Given the codebase's deliberate accept-ValueError-on-degenerate-input contract, this is best classified not-a-bug rather than a true latent defect.

refuted.title: Corrupt matrix row / channel id in config string raises uncaught ValueError during PFile parse
refuted.reasoning: The code is accurately described: p_file.py:249 does `[int(x) for x in val.split()]` for matrix rows and p_file.py:390 does `[int(x) for x in ch['id'].replace(',', ' ').split()]`, and I reproduced the `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'` from `parse_config('[matrix]\nrow1 = 1 abc 3\n')`. But the defect does not hold as stated, for several converging reasons. (1) The trigger requires a CORRUPT config string with a non-integer matrix row or channel id. That config is hardware/firmware-generated embedded data in the RSI .p binary (TN-051 record 0), not external free-form user input. There is no valid run where this fires; the finding itself rates it 'nit'/'medium' and frames it as an untrusted-input robustness gap on corruption. (2) The finding's core complaint — that the ValueError 'lacks file/line context' so 'batch processing can't report which file is malformed' — is factually false against the actual callers. convert.py:605-606 and 615-617 catch `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` and log WITH the filename (`logger.error(f"{p_path.name}: {e}")`); perturb/pipeline.py:938-940 wraps `PFile(p_path)` in `try/except Exception` and logs `logger.error("loading %s: %s", p_path.name, exc)` then continues. So the offending file IS already reported in the two batch/production paths; only the token within the file is omitted, a cosmetic message nicety. (3) Not a port-faithfulness regression: the ODAS reference (setupstr.m:357 `C = textscan(row{1}, '%d')`) is itself not robust to non-numeric matrix tokens and adds no file/line context, so this code matches reference behavior. The only unguarded default-invoked path is the diagnostic `rsi info` subcommand (cli.py:234), where a traceback would still show the line and (in practice) the path; this is a metadata-print command, not the primary epsilon/chi path, and never fires on valid hardware data. Net: a latent, corruption-only edge with the batch paths already supplying filename context and reference parity — the suggested fix is a cosmetic error-message enhancement, not a defect. Classifying as not-a-bug.

refuted.title: Bottom-crash trim silently skipped when detected crash depth is at/above the segment's first retained slow sample (caller off-by-one, aggravated by PR #78 M4)
refuted.reasoning: Code at pipeline.py:433-438 reads exactly as the finding quotes: `P_seg = P_slow[new_s:e_slow+1]; below = np.where(P_seg >= bottom_depth)[0]; if len(below)>0: candidate = new_s + int(below[0]) - 1; if candidate > new_s: new_e = candidate`. The mechanism is correctly described: when below[0]==0 (and even below[0]==1), candidate <= new_s, the guard fails, and no bottom trim is applied. So as a pure code statement the latent off-by-one exists. However it does NOT matter in practice on this branch. (1) Reachability: below[0]==0 requires P_slow[new_s] >= bottom_depth, i.e. the SHALLOWEST retained sample of the profile is already at/below the crash depth. bottom_depth is the sample-mean of the DEEPEST vibration-spiking bin (detect_bottom_crash in processing/bottom.py:124-136 searches deepest-bin-upward and returns the mean of samples in that bin). On any real descending cast that depth is near the bottom, far deeper than the profile start P_slow[new_s]. The finding's ONLY reproduction (P_slow=[40,42,44,46,48], bottom_depth=39) requires the crash to be shallower than the entire profile, which is physically impossible for a deepest-bin crash detection on a descending profile. (2) The claimed PR #78 / M4 interaction is unsubstantiated and backwards: M4 (bottom.py:126-136) changed the return from bin geometric center to bin sample-mean. The flagged bin is the deepest spiking bin, so its sample mean is in the DEEP region, not near the shallow profile start; M4 only moves the depth shallower than the bin center (which previously overhung BELOW nanmax(depth)) to WITHIN the observed depths — keeping it deep. The explicit purpose of M4, per its own code comment, was to make the caller's `P >= bottom_depth` MATCH (it matched nothing ~half the time before), i.e. M4 makes trimming MORE likely to fire, not less, and does not push the reported depth toward new_s. So M4 does not increase the chance of the below[0]==0 no-trim path. The existing test (test_perturb_pipeline.py:1477-1507) covers the realistic crash-at-95%-depth path and trims correctly. The off-by-one is a latent boundary nit with no realistic trigger on valid descending-profile data; it neither corrupts nor loses real output on any valid run, so real=false / not-a-bug.

refuted.title: top_trim silently skips length-mismatched channels instead of aborting, inconsistent with bottom-crash
refuted.reasoning: The factual claims are accurate: top_trim.py:81-82 silently `continue`s a channel whose len != len(depth), while bottom.py:82-83 does `if len(a) != len(depth): return None` to abort. So the described inconsistency exists. However, this is not a defect on this branch: (1) The finding self-rates "nit" and its own evidence concedes "Neither is wrong" — it is purely a cross-helper style inconsistency, not a bug. (2) It is unreachable in the only real production caller: perturb/pipeline.py:386-394 builds fast_channels by slicing every channel with the identical `[s_fast:e_fast]` slice used for `depth_seg = P_fast[s_fast:e_fast]`, so all channels are guaranteed equal length; the mismatch branch only fires on deliberate caller misuse. (3) The skip behavior is the documented, intentional contract — compute_trim_depth's docstring (lines 53-55) states each channel "must match depth_fast in length," and tests/test_processing_top_trim.py:65 (test_mismatched_channel_length_skipped) explicitly asserts the skip is by design. (4) Even if triggered, there is no crash, no silent corruption, and no wrong primary result — at worst the trim uses fewer channels, given the caller passed a malformed input. (5) PR #78 reworked bottom.py (the comment block at 126-136 is part of the audit fixes) and deliberately kept its `return None` on mismatch, so the inconsistent state is reviewed and intentional. No defect that matters in practice; this is a latent cosmetic consistency observation at most.

refuted.title: Undeclared `pandas` runtime dependency crashes documented GPS-CSV and hotel-CSV/ISO ingestion on a clean install
refuted.reasoning: The code-level facts are accurate: pandas is imported unguarded at gps.py:214 (inside GPSFromCSV.__init__, reached via create_gps when gps.source=="csv", a documented option at config.py:326) and at hotel.py:177/186/285 (reached via load_hotel -> _load_csv / _parse_time for .csv files and time_format=="iso"/non-numeric, dispatched from pipeline.py:1884-1886). I confirmed pyproject.toml `dependencies` (lines 16-25) lists only numpy/netCDF4/scipy/xarray/gsw/ruamel.yaml/matplotlib/cmocean — pandas is NOT explicitly declared. HOWEVER, the finding's load-bearing impact claim — that a fresh `pip install microstructure-tpw` raises `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'` — is false. `xarray>=2024.1` IS in `dependencies`, and xarray declares `pandas>=2.2` as a CORE (non-extra) requirement; I verified this directly via `importlib.metadata.requires('xarray')`, which lists `'pandas>=2.2'` with no `extra ==` marker (unconditional), alongside the extra-gated entries. pandas has always been a hard runtime dependency of xarray, not optional. Therefore pip will always transitively install pandas, and `import pandas` succeeds (verified: pandas 3.0.1 importable in this env, gps module imports cleanly). The claimed crash on documented configs cannot occur. The only valid residual point is packaging hygiene: a package should explicitly declare a library it imports directly rather than relying on a transitive dependency (the dep could vanish if xarray is dropped/replaced). That is a latent cosmetic/cleanliness nit, not the major silent-corruption/crash defect claimed. PR #78 (chi/profile/ct_align/bottom fixes) is unrelated to this and does not bear on it. Real=false because the asserted user-visible failure does not happen.

refuted.title: Deconvolution runs before unsigned-wrap, reversing ODAS order — but no real channel is both unsigned and deconvolved, so latent only
refuted.reasoning:
The finding's technical ordering claim is factually CORRECT on both sides. (1) ODAS: read_odas.m (called at odas_p2mat.m:372) applies the sign/unsigned correction at lines 388-390 during the read, BEFORE deconvolve runs in odas_p2mat.m at line 566. (2) Python p_file.py: _apply_deconvolution() is invoked at line 498, and the sign=='unsigned'/jac_t wrap loop runs afterward at lines 503-523 — the reverse order. So the port order is genuinely flipped, and for a hypothetical channel that is simultaneously sign=unsigned, has diff_gain, follows X_dX naming, is a single-id fast int16 channel (still signed >i2/<i2 at line 440-446 at decon time), and whose raw counts actually cross the int16 wrap, the deconvolution would see a spurious sign discontinuity ODAS never sees.

HOWEVER, this cannot affect any real run on this branch, so the practical-impact bar is not met. I dumped the actual ARCTERX instrument config (tests/data/SN479_0006.p, the default data path): the ONLY deconvolved channels (diff_gain + X_dX naming) are T1_dT1, T2_dT2 (type therm) and P_dP (type poly) — all SIGNED, no sign=unsigned field. The ONLY unsigned channels are JAC_C (type jac_c) and JAC_T (type jac_t) — neither has diff_gain nor X_dX naming, so neither is deconvolved. The {deconvolved} and {unsigned} channel sets are disjoint; the bug's trigger conditions are never co-satisfied.

The finding's sole cited unsigned-and-deconvolved example, micro-conductivity C1_dC1, is hypothetical: (a) ODAS read_odas.m:383 explicitly SKIPS jac_c from the sign loop entirely (continue), so ODAS never wraps it either; (b) rsijac_c/jac_c are 32-bit Q3.28 fixed-point channels (division_factor=2^28 per convert_odas.m:274), not int16 channels that wrap at 32767; (c) no config, test fixture, ARCTERX file, or ODAS reference in the repo defines any micro-conductivity channel as a sign=unsigned int16 X_dX pre-emphasized/deconvolved channel. The finding itself self-labels confidence medium and the order as 'latently wrong.'

The reorder is a reasonable defensive port-faithfulness fix, but on this branch it corrupts no real output. Original 'major' (silent corruption of real output) is overstated since no real channel is affected; correct severity is nit (latent, zero current practical impact). real=false because the defect does not genuinely matter in practice on this branch.

refuted.title: Deconvolve propagates a single NaN across the entire channel via lfilter with no guard (same failure class as the PR #78 M3 ct_align fix, left uncovered in this path)
refuted.reasoning: The described mechanism is technically real and I verified it: feeding a single NaN to scipy.signal.lfilter poisons every subsequent output sample (confirmed empirically — NaN at input index 5 corrupts output indices 5..end). deconvolve.py has no finite-input guard at lines 65/72/82/95, and the PR #78 M3 ct_align fix (ct_align.py:83 `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue`) does establish that pattern elsewhere. HOWEVER, the finding does not hold as a real defect on this branch: (1) The sole production caller is p_file.py:588, `deconvolve(X_raw, X_dX_raw, ...)`, where both args come from self.channels_raw, which is populated (p_file.py:448/473) from raw_flat int16 binary file data. Integer arrays cannot contain NaN, so the production path can never trigger the smear. The finding itself concedes 'In the normal PFile flow deconvolve receives raw int16 counts (cannot be NaN), so the live risk is low.' (2) I searched every caller in src/ and tests/: the only invocation outside tests is p_file.py:588. The finding's claim that deconvolve is 'callable on float64 hres / hotel-file data' is unsubstantiated — fp07_cal.py (line 333) reads the already-deconvolved channels_raw and applies calibration; it does NOT call deconvolve. 'Hotel-file data' is merged into pf.channels for speed computation (speed.py, helpers.py) and never flows through deconvolve. There is no real code path that passes NaN-capable float data into this function. (3) The ct_align analogy does not transfer: ct_align operates on physical-unit float64 T/C that realistically can be NaN (so its guard fixes a reachable path), whereas deconvolve's only real caller passes integer counts. The finding is self-classified as a latent 'defense-in-depth gap.' Severity 'nit' is correct (latent/cosmetic-hardening), but real=false because no reachable input path can trigger the defect on this branch — it is a hypothetical hardening suggestion, not a manifest bug.

refuted.title: Cross-spectrum DC/Nyquist halving is symmetric in Python but asymmetric in MATLAB reference (deliberate divergence; 2 edge bins differ)
refuted.reasoning: The factual core of the finding is accurate. spectral.py:247-250 in the cross case loops `for arr in (Cxx, Cyy, Cxy): arr/=norm; arr[0]/=2; arr[-1]/=2`, symmetrically halving both DC and Nyquist for all three matrices. The MATLAB reference odas/csd_matrix_odas.m halves Cxy at both ends (lines 252-253) but Cxx only at DC (line 257 `Cxx(1,:,:)/2`, no Cxx(end)) and Cyy only at Nyquist (line 258 `Cyy(end,:,:)/2`, no Cyy(1)). So Python additionally halves Cxx's Nyquist bin and Cyy's DC bin — exactly the two-bin divergence described. However this is NOT a behavioral defect on this branch: (1) The symmetric halving is the physically/Parseval-correct convention; the MATLAB asymmetry is the inconsistency. (2) The divergent bins have negligible impact: Cxx=UU is the shear auto-spectrum whose Nyquist bin is never reached by the dissipation integral — l4.py bounds all integration/fits by K_limit = min(K_AA, K_LIMIT_MAX=150) where K_AA=0.9*f_AA/W is the anti-alias cutoff well below Nyquist (l4.py:374, 510-511), and the initial estimate only integrates to K_INITIAL_CUTOFF=10 cpm (l4.py:362,366). Cyy=AA is the accel auto-spectrum feeding the Goodman solve at f=0, but the variance and ISR fits both exclude the DC bin (qc_idx = np.arange(1,...) at l4.py:399; fits use spec[1:...] at l4.py:488-489), and linear detrend (goodman.py:150) drives the DC bin toward zero anyway. (3) This finding does not target any of PR #78's four fixed files (chi/chi.py, rsi/profile.py, processing/ct_align.py, processing/bottom.py); the audit fix commit 1a78fca does not touch spectral.py. The finding itself states 'No code change needed' and is 'flagging only for traceability.' I confirmed no documenting comment currently exists near 247-250. This is a latent/cosmetic traceability note about a deliberate, more-correct divergence with no measurable effect on the primary result — real=false, severity nit.

refuted.title: Python Kraichnan gradient spectrum uses a different functional form than the in-repo MATLAB reference (and standard literature), biasing kB/epsilon_T/chi on the DEFAULT chi path
refuted.reasoning: The finding's factual observations are correct: batchelor.py:177 implements S = chi*q/(kappa_T*kB**2)*k*exp(-sqrt(6q)*y) (simple exponential), while matlab/kraichnan_gradT.m:26-27 uses S = chi*q/(3*kappa_T*kB^2)*k*(1+sqrt(6q)*y)*exp(-sqrt(6q)*y) (extra 1/3 normalization + (1+sqrt(6q)y) polynomial). I numerically confirmed both integrate to chi/(6*kappa_T) (ratio 1.0000), the peaks differ 17.8 vs 28.8 cpm (1.618x), and the shape ratio swings 2.34x->0.25x over y=0.05..2.0 — all the finding's numbers reproduce, and kraichnan IS the default model (rsi/config.py:54, perturb/config.py:119). BUT the finding's central premise — that the (1+sqrt(6q)y) MATLAB form is the literature-standard and the Python simple-exponential is the biasing deviation — is INVERTED. The authoritative oceanographic literature gives the 1D temperature-GRADIENT Kraichnan spectrum as the simple-exponential form: the ametsoc/JPO sources (Sanchez/Roget et al. 2011 'Batchelor and Kraichnan Models'; Scheifele et al. 2018 JGR) state F(k) = chi*q^(1/2)*y_k*exp(-sqrt(6)*y_k) / F_z^T(k_z)=chi*q_K^(1/2)*y_k*exp(-sqrt(6)*sqrt(y_k)*k_T/k_B) — linear-in-y prefactor, single exponential, NO (1+sqrt(6q)y) polynomial. That is exactly the PYTHON form. The (1+...) factor is the 3D-scalar transform artifact, not the 1D gradient spectrum (implied 1D scalar of the MATLAB form is (1/k + sqrt(6q)/kB)exp, vs the literature 1/k*exp). PR #54 commit abb1930 deliberately made this correction: its message states 'Bogucki et al. (1997) eq. 11 is q*chi/(kappa_T*kB^2)*k*exp(-sqrt(6q)k/kB); the previous form kept a (1+sqrt(6q)y) factor from the 3-D spectrum' — i.e. the Python was fixed TO the correct form and the MATLAB reference was simply not updated. Therefore on this branch the default chi path uses the literature-correct spectrum and is NOT biased; the only residual is a stale matlab/kraichnan_gradT.m (a doc/consistency nit in a non-shipping reference file). The finding's suggested fix (restore the (1+...) form in Python) would actively REINTRODUCE the bias. real=false; not-a-bug for the Python product.

refuted.title: Variance-correction returns NaN (no correction) for high-kB / tiny-K_max windows where the old grid produced a finite value -- a behavioral regression from PR #78
refuted.reasoning:
The mechanism is real and I reproduced it: PR #78 (diff vs main confirmed) replaced the old grid `K_fine=linspace(K_upper/n_fine*0.01, K_upper)` (first point ~kB*2.5e-5) with `K_fine = arange(1,n+1)*(K_upper/n)` where the first grid point is K_upper/n = (40*kB)/(40*2000) = kB/2000 (chi.py:112-114). When K_max < kB/2000 the mask `(K_fine <= min(K_max,K_upper))` selects nothing and the new `if not np.any(mask): return np.nan` guard (line 126-127) fires. Verified numerically: kB=2240,K_max=0.7 -> nan; kB=4000,K_max=1.0 -> nan; old grid returned a finite value for the same inputs. So the behavioral-regression claim is technically accurate.

But it does not hold ON THIS BRANCH in practice, and the severity is overstated:

(1) Mis-cited impact for Method 1. At chi.py:261 (_chi_from_epsilon) the correction is used ONLY to compute chi_vc, which is merely the CENTER of a 4-decade-wide log-spaced grid search (chi_lo=chi_vc*0.01, chi_hi=chi_vc*100, 200 points, lines 268-270); the final chi comes from the log-space least-squares fit, not from the correction. A NaN correction just falls back to chi_vc=6*KAPPA_T*obs_var (line 265), shifting the grid center, with the fit still recovering the right chi. So the finding's 'under-estimating chi' does not apply to Method 1.

(2) Unreachable for the two paths where it WOULD matter (chi.py:440 _mle_fit and chi.py:588 _iterative_fit, where chi=chi_band*correction directly). In both, K_max is the last above-noise wavenumber K[valid_idx[-1]], and both gate at min_points=6 (chi.py:343-344 and 517-520). With the default fft_length=1024 and fs_fast~512 Hz at speed~0.7 m/s, dK=fs/nfft/speed~0.714 cpm, so the 6-point gate forces K_max >= K[6]~4.29 cpm. The NaN condition K_max < kB/2000 therefore requires kB > ~8571 cpm, i.e. epsilon > ~0.1 W/kg -- a regime essentially never present in profiling data, and self-contradictory (a genuine kB~8500 cpm spectrum has above-noise signal far above 4 cpm, so the MLE would never fit such a kB to a spectrum that dies by K[6]). kB=2240 (the finding's case) corresponds to epsilon~1e-3 with K_max needing to be <1.12 cpm, geometrically impossible past the 6-bin gate.

(3) Even if reached, the fallback (lines 262-265, 448-454, 591-594) drops the correction without crashing or corrupting -- no silent loss of real output on any valid run. The finding itself rates it minor and concedes 'usually fail the min_points>=6 gate earlier'; in fact with default config the gate ALWAYS prevents the NaN for any physically realizable kB. This is a latent edge case in synthetic-input space only, so real=false; if anything it is a nit, not a minor.

refuted.title: read_config_text silently returns garbage/truncated config on a corrupt header_size or oversized config_size
refuted.reasoning: REFUTED as a silent-data-corruption defect; downgraded to a latent nit. Claim 1 (read_config_text returns unvalidated garbage) is literally true in isolation: I reproduced both cases. A file advertising header_size=20 makes read_config_text return 40 bytes of binary header decoded latin-1 as "config" (matches the evidence repr exactly); a file with config_size=5000 overrunning EOF returns only the 203 available bytes with no error. BUT claim 2 — the load-bearing part that makes this "silent-data-corruption" — is FALSE on this branch: the asserted flow patch_files -> edit_config_text -> write_patched_pfile does NOT propagate garbage into patched output. write_patched_pfile (config_patch.py:498-525) independently re-reads the source header and re-derives header_size/config_size (lines 503-505), so it never byte-copies the string returned by read_config_text. Its existing guard at lines 516-525 raises ValueError on the EOF-overrun case ("header_size+config_size (5128) is not smaller than the file (331 bytes); the source is truncated or has no data records") — I reproduced this, no output written. The corrupt header_size<128 case raises at struct.pack_into (line 528, buffer-too-small) before any write. So no truncated/garbage config can silently become the active patched config. The only unguarded residual is read_config_text used standalone at line 553 inside scaffold_yaml — a non-destructive YAML-template generator the user reviews and edits; parse_config renders garbage as empty sections (verified: matrix=[], instrument_info={}), an obviously broken template, not silent corruption of scientific output. Furthermore the primary scientific data path PFile._read (p_file.py:311-312) does the identical unvalidated seek(header_size)/read(config_size) and has processed 30 real ARCTERX files fine; real instrument files carry valid headers. Not introduced/affected by PR #78. At most a nit (input-validation hardening on a debug/inspect helper); not a real practical defect on this branch.

refuted.title: Bottom-crash trim keeps the shallow ~half of the spike bin (sample-mean depth still leaves seafloor-impact-contaminated data in epsilon/chi)
refuted.reasoning:
REFUTED. I read bottom.py:124-136 (the PR #78 M4 fix), the caller perturb/pipeline.py:432-438, top_trim.py:72-99, tests/test_processing_bottom.py, and the M4 git diff vs main.

(1) The finding's central premise — "the bottom CRASH onset is at the SHALLOWEST edge of the spike bin" — is asserted, not supported, and is physically backwards for a descending profiler. The crash is an impulsive impact at the DEEPEST samples (seafloor contact at profile end); that deep impulse is what drives the bin's std above threshold. The shallow-half samples within that same bin are normal descent data, not crash-contaminated. So switching to nanmin (shallow edge) would discard ~2 m of GOOD data, not contaminated data; the claimed HIGH bias to epsilon/chi from "retaining" the shallow half is unestablished.

(2) The suggested fix reintroduces the exact under-read the codebase deliberately rejected. nanmin over the in-bin samples ≈ bins[i] = the shallow/left edge. bottom.py's own comment (lines 126-134) and top_trim.py:72,99 both document the codebase's stance (issue #22): the shallow edge UNDER-reads the depth, so both top_trim and bottom-crash use bin centers. The test test_reports_sample_mean_within_flagged_bin (lines 27-47) explicitly asserts the M4 result is ~the bin center AND within observed samples — the deliberate compromise (avoid #22 under-read, while guaranteeing the value stays ≤ nanmax(depth) so the caller's `P >= bottom_depth` always trims). The finding treats the shallow edge as the conservative ideal, contradicting this documented/tested decision.

(3) Magnitude is overstated and immaterial. The nanmean-vs-nanmin delta is ≤ ~2 m, only at the very deepest extreme of the profile. A single dissipation window already spans ~4 m (diss_length default = 4*fft_length = 2048 fast samples ≈ 4 m at ~0.7 m/s; final output dz = 0.5 m). At most ~1 segment at the profile's deepest edge — the region analysts already treat with caution — could differ. This is not silent corruption/loss of real output (minor as scoped requires that).

(4) Not a PR #78 regression: M4 strictly improved on the prior bin-center (which could fall below the deepest sample and silently fail to trim, ~half of detections). This finding asks to push past the fix in a direction the codebase rejected, with a fix that is plausibly worse. All 11 bottom tests pass on this branch. Verdict: not-a-bug (debatable edge-of-profile tuning preference, suggested fix likely harmful).

refuted.title: numpy scalar config values pass through normalization unconverted and crash json.dumps in compute_hash
refuted.reasoning: The described mechanics are factually correct and I verified them: _normalize_value in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py special-cases bool/int/float/str and falls through to `return v` at line 41 for anything else; np.int64 is not a Python int instance (isinstance(np.int64(256), int) == False, confirmed at runtime), so it passes through unconverted and json.dumps(np.int64) raises `TypeError: Object of type int64 is not JSON serializable` (also confirmed at runtime). np.float64 subclasses float and is handled. HOWEVER the defect is unreachable on this branch and the finding itself admits this ('Currently NOT reachable', confidence: low, category: robustness/latent). Every caller of canonicalize/compute_hash/resolve_output_dir/merge_config (rsi/cli.py:182,202; perturb/pipeline.py:165-179,1637-1673,1990-2025,2133-2167; perturb/cli.py) feeds params derived exclusively from (a) YAML config loaded via ruamel.yaml -> native int/float/str, (b) argparse CLI args -> native Python types, or (c) the hardcoded DEFAULTS dicts in perturb/config.py and rsi/config.py, which I grepped and contain no numpy values (gps.lat/gps.lon default to None). No code path injects a numpy scalar into config hashing. The only postulated trigger ('a future geospatial auto-fill feeding gps.lat from a numpy array') is hypothetical future code, not present behavior. PR #78 does not touch config_base.py and is irrelevant here. This is a purely latent/defensive gap that cannot manifest in any current run, so per the rubric (real=true only if the defect genuinely holds and matters in practice on this branch) I set real=false. Severity, if it were ever reachable, would be at most nit (latent robustness); it is not a blocker/major because no real run can hit it.

refuted.title: visc() Sharqawy branch lacks the negative-viscosity floor that visc35 has, crashing nasmyth on a spurious-cold window mean
refuted.reasoning: The finding's two narrow facts are correct but its trigger is physically unreachable on this branch, so the "major / crashes the whole file" claim does not hold. Confirmed facts: (a) the Sharqawy branch returns mu/rho with no floor (ocean.py:91) while visc35 floors at 1.0e-7 (ocean.py:37); (b) visc(-42.0,34,100) = -1.24e-4 (negative) and nasmyth raises ValueError on negative nu (nasmyth.py:36-37). However, the crash requires a FINITE temperature below ~-41.9 C reaching visc, and that cannot occur. Both production paths source temperature exclusively from the FP07 T1 channel: epsilon path l3.temp <- l1.temp <- pf.channels['T1'] (adapter.py:74-76, l3.py:179, dissipation.py:218); chi path T_means <- l2_chi.temp <- l1.temp (l2_chi.py:135-136, l3_chi.py:163). T1 runs through convert_therm (channels.py:89-129), which HARD-CLAMPS Z to [-0.6,0.6] (line 119) => R_ratio in [0.25,4.0] => with typical coeffs (T_0=289 K, beta_1=3000) output temperature is bounded to [-18.2 C, +60.4 C]. The coldest physically-producible FP07 value (~-18 C) is ~24 C above the Sharqawy denominator zero-crossing at -41.9 C, so nu can never go negative via the real data path. The finding's three posited triggers all fail: -99 CF _FillValue is masked to NaN (profile.py:331), and NaN nu does NOT crash (np.nan<=0 is False -> nasmyth returns NaN, verified) so it merely NaNs that window; a frozen/dropped-out thermistor yields NaN (handled) or a clamped rail >= -18 C; a corrupt L3 temp is an np.mean of clamped FP07 degC values. The chi path additionally substitutes 10 C for non-finite window temps (l3_chi.py:169-175) and uses np.nanmean. Crucially, the visc35 floor closed a REACHABLE hazard, not a symmetric one: the FP07 HOT rail reaches +71 to +121 C for beta_1<=2500 (verified), exceeding visc35's ~64.5 C zero-crossing, so the hot-side crash was real; the cold side the finding posits is unreachable because the FP07 cold rail bottoms ~24 C short of -42 C. The missing floor is at most latent defense-in-depth (a nit), not the major file-aborting crash described, and cannot fire on any valid run on this branch.
